point clouds [31]. Bleyer et al. [32] argued that the common
local stereo assumption fails to handle slanted surfaces,
resulting in a bias toward reconstructing front-parallel surfaces.
To address this limitation, they proposed PatchMatch Stereo,
which seeks to identify an optimal slanted support plane for
each pixel. Xu et al. [33] further observed that each object
can be characterized by three aspects: its perimeter in image
space, the slanted depth plane, and the planar bias. They
introduced a unified variational formulation for joint object
segmentation and stereo matching. Yin et al. [34] proposed
the use of surface normals to enhance geometric consistency
in regular structures. By associating depth planes with multiple
coefficients, Yang and Zhou [35] proposed a parametric
plane representation that facilitates network learning and
leads to more accurate scene geometry recovery. A similar
representation involving plane coefficients is utilized in [36]
to guide the depth-decoder during upsampling. To leverage
this prior knowledge, Ramamonjisoa et al. [37] trained offset
vectors to identify centers of instance segmentation from
annotated images. Patil et al. [38] optimized plane coefficients
along with spatial offset vectors to identify coplanar pixels and
exploit this coplanarity for depth prediction, resulting in state-
of-the-art performance.
Planarity prior has demonstrated their effectiveness in
various image processing tasks. However, there has been
limited exploration of it in LF depth estimation. In this
paper, we thoroughly investigate the effectiveness of planarity
prior in LF depth estimation and propose a novel surface-
continuous scene representation that leverages this prior.
This representation addresses the limitations associated with
discrete image representation.
III. SURFACE-CONTINUOUS SCENE REPRESENTATION
A. Motivation
The real-world surface is continuous, whereas its image
representation becomes discrete due to the limited resolution
of the camera, as illustrated in Fig. 1-A. This discrete scene
representation reflects the incompleteness of the training data,
which prevents a supervised learning-based network from
accurately modeling the depth distribution of a real scene using
only training images.
This issue becomes particularly pronounced when dealing
with light field (LF) data. As depicted in Fig. 2-a, a light

IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS FOR VIDEO TECHNOLOGY, VOL. 35, NO. 5, MAY 2025
field (LF) captures both spatial and angular information of
a scene. The ray originating from a 3D point is determined
by the intersection points of two parallel planes ( and 5),
denoted as L(x, y, u, v). Here, L represents the LF, while
(u, v) and (x, y) denote the angular and spatial coordinates,
respectively. The captured data can be organized as a U × V
array of sub-aperture images (SAIs) with dimensions H × W
(Fig. 2-b). Such dense angular sampling (U × V ) results
in an unavoidable decrease in spatial resolution H × W,
further exacerbating the discretization issue. Based on the
aforementioned analysis, it becomes more challenging for
learning models to accurately capture the depth distribution of
the real scene using LF images. While image super-resolution
technology can mitigate the effects of discretization, it still
produces discrete information. Therefore, there is a need to
reconstruct continuous scene signals from discrete images for
LF depth estimation tasks.
However,
this
is
an
ill-posed
problem
due
to
the
irreversibility of discretization. Fortunately, we have observed
that utilizing the planarity prior provides a solution to this
problem. Taking advantage of the high regularity found in the
real world, the planarity prior assumes that the scene surface
is locally flat, allowing us to reconstruct locally continuous
surface signals in the form of planes. Details will be introduced
below.
B. Definition of Continuous Surface
The real surface is continuous, but its representation in
images is discrete, as illustrated in Fig. 3-a and b. For ease
of explanation, We define a 3D coordinate system (x, y, z
axes and origin O), where x Oy is the plane where image
img ∈RH×W is located, and the z-axis represents its pixel
value, (Generally speaking, this value should be the depth
value, but in supervised learning-based depth estimation task,
we can also extend it to rgb value due to the correspondence
between color image and depth map.) Then in this system, the
discrete image representation Rdis
img is composed of H × W
pixels pxyz.
Now for network to learn an accurate depth distribution and
make robust predictions, we must utilize Rdis
img to construct
a continuous representation of real-world surfaces. However,
this problem is inherently ill-posed without any constraints.
Fortunately, the high regularity exhibited by 3D objects allows
us to make assumptions about the scene structure, that is
piecewise planarity prior. This prior assumes that within a
localized region, the object surface can be regarded as a
plane. This assumption holds true in most scenes, and given
that planes are continuous, it is evident that we can utilize
this prior to reconstruct the continuous surface information
from Rdis
img.
However, when utilized excessively in depth estimation,
this prior knowledge can have counterproductive effects,
as demonstrated in Fig. 3-c. Earlier approaches commonly
assume that the surfaces localized to objects are planes, which
can significantly compromise the high-frequency information
(indicated by black points). In this work, we refine the previous
object-local plane assumption to a pixel-wise representation.
This refinement enables us to preserve the original image
information entirely and fully leverage the benefits of the
planarity prior. As depicted in Fig. 3-d, we can transform Rdis
img
into a continuous plane representation Rcont
pla by constructing
planes F between adjacent pixel-points p:

Now we possess a continuous signal representation Rcont

the scene surface. However, enabling the network to learn from

To solve it, a novel Plane Regular Sampling Operator (PRSO)
is proposed.

D. Deeper Insights Into the Representation
Here, we provide a comprehensive analysis of our surface-
continuous scene representation and highlight its inherent
advantages for LF depth estimation to facilitate better
understanding. For ease of explanation, we first explain Rcont
img ,
and then explain its expansion in feature map.
1) Continuous Image Representation Rcont
img : We explain the
role of Rcont
img in LF depth estimation from data and loss
constraint perspectives respectively. From data, Rcont
img is an
expansion of original data Rdis
img (more data leads to better
performance). The proposed operator, FPRSO, is similar to
the currently popular data augmentation operation, MixUP.
However, previous MixUP can only handle image-level tasks
(classification) and cannot handle dense predictions due to
their destruction of semantics. FPRSO is an innovative
extension of MixUP in dense regression prediction. From
loss constraint, Rcont
img introduces planarity prior, which has a
potential smoothing effect on predicted depth map, similar to
smooth loss and normal loss. However, compared with using
loss, Rcont
img does not need to balance the weights of different
losses, which means that it has better applicability.
2) Feature Extension: Applying FPRSO to feature level
is somewhat abstract, but it is a very meaningful thing,
which is similar to feature regularization and deep supervision
technology. For the former, Mainfold MiXUP mixes network
intermediate features in batches, which makes classification
boundary smoother and the distribution learned more rea-
sonable, while feature FPRSO is an innovation of Mainfold
MiXUP in dense prediction tasks. For the latter, Deep
supervision achieves multi-scale deep loss supervision by
adding multiple auxiliary prediction layers in backbone.
Applying FPRSO to multiple intermediate feature maps can
indirectly realize deep feature supervision in network middle
layers. And due to the parameter-free, plug-and-play nature
of FPRSO, there is no need to weigh the weights of multiple
deep losses, better than using deep loss.
3) Summary: In this section, we propose a novel surface-
continuous scene representation using planarity prior to solve
the problem of discrete scene information. To avoid destroying
the original image information, we refine plane assumption to
pixel one, then present a novel plane regular sampling operator
to approximate it. The final achieved Rcont
pix has the following
advantages: 1) Network can directly mine the true depth
distribution from it without complex planarization processing.
2) Its sample Rcont
img can be directly learned by any existing LF
depth estimation network without any change. 3) The original
LF image information Rdis
img is completely retained in Rcont
pix .
IV. PLANE-BASED NETWORK FOR LIGHT FIELD
DEPTH ESTIMATION
Despite effectively performing depth inference on unseen
scenes by leveraging the intrinsic correlation between LF
images and ground-truth depth. However, due to images being
discretized representations of scene information, learning from
them does not enable the network to accurately capture the true
depth distribution. In other words, it is not feasible to learn the
true depth of a real scene solely from discretized information.

Therefore, the core of our idea is to reconstruct continuous
scene surfaces using planarity prior and make the network
learn it, as discussed in Sec. III. As the representation Rcont
img
sampled through FPRSO maintains data structure consistency
with the original image, we construct our network based
on previous work. In this regard, we employ DepthDF
[39] as the backbone and introduce PlaneNet, a Plane-
based Network specifically designed for LF depth estimation.
Through the integration of FPRSO, PlaneNet can accomplish
both the sampling and learning of surface-continuous scene
representations throughout the training process. Additionally,
thanks to FPRSO’s parameter-free and real-time sampling
capabilities, PlaneNet achieves enhanced accuracy compared
to the backbone network without incurring any additional
computational cost. Details are introduced below.
A. System Overview
Fig. 4 illustrates the architecture of the proposed PlaneNet,
which comprises two main components: the backbone network
and the planar sampling module. The backbone of PlaneNet
employs DepthDF [39] for LF depth estimation (additionally,
we also design more backbone networks in Sec. V-E for proof).
DepthDF takes as input four stacked LF images captured
from different orientations: horizontal (E0◦), vertical(E90◦),
right diagonal(E45◦), and left diagonal(E135◦). Each stack has
dimensions of C×H×W×N (batch is neglected for simplicity,
where N denotes the number of stacked images). The resulting
output is the center-view depth map, referred to as Dpre.
We ensure consistency between the backbone of PlaneNet and
DepthDF.
The planar sampling module is specifically designed for
sampling Rcont
img , where Rcont
img refers to D′
gt,l f ′. It is integrated
into the backbone network using a progressive learning
strategy and operates solely during the training phase. The
module utilizes FPRSO to sample the continuous surface
signal from the input images L as well as the intermediate
features l f . During testing, the module is deactivated to align
with the target input.
B. Backbone Network
Our proposed surface-continuous scene representation can
be effortlessly integrated into existing work through the
utilization of FPRSO, ensuring a seamless integration process.
We select DepthDF [39] as the backbone for two reasons.
Firstly, DepthDF has demonstrated exceptional performance
in LF depth estimation, making it an ideal candidate for
further validating our research. Secondly, DepthDF focuses
on studying the depth continuity of scene while disregarding
surface continuity. In contrast, our work addresses this
limitation and represents a significant advancement in this
aspect.
For ease of understanding, we briefly introduce DepthDF
here. As shown in Fig. 4-backbone, DepthDF consists of four
modules, namely Encoder, Predictor, Sampler and Constrainer.
The input to it are four stacked LF images captured from
different orientations: horizontal (E0◦), vertical(E90◦), right

diagonal(E45◦), and left diagonal(E135◦). The output is the
depth map of LF center view Dpre. The specific details of
each module are:
Encoder: Robust geometric features are crucial for LF depth
estimation, and the encoder in DepthDF is used to process
the input four image stacks. It first uses 2DCNN to extract
features of each stack, then concatenates them and use 2DCNN
for fusion. Finally, the LF depth feature l f ∈RC×H×W is
achieved.
Predictor: With l f , the predictor in DepthDF is performed
to predict depth Dpre ∈R1×H×W directly, which consists of
two 2D Residual blocks and one Conv2d layer.
With Encoder and Predictor, the scene depth map can be
obtained in an end-to-end way. Besides this, DepthDF also
studies depth continuity of 3D scene, that is, along the depth
direction, the distance between depth point da and db equals a
signed number (da - db). Therefore, in addition to estimating
depth map, DepthDF also predicts the distance map using
Sampler and Constrainer.
Sampler: The sampler in DepthDF is used for data
preparation, including distance map Vgt and query map Q.
Each pixel value in Q is depth point sampled between ground
truth and predicted depth map Dpre, and Vgt is the distance
from Q to ground truth.
Constrainer: The input of constrainer is feature map l f
output by encoder and query map Q. It uses Conv1D to
process the corresponding pixels on l f and Q, then predicts
the distance map Vpre.
When training, PlaneNet calculates losses of Vpre and
Vgt, Dpre and Dgt respectively, and updates parameters by
combining these two losses.
In addition to PlaneNet, we also design anther network for
proof by changing the backbone in Sec. V-E.
C. Planar Sampling Module
DepthDF has achieved remarkable performance in LF depth
estimation by effectively modeling depth-continuous scenes.
Nonetheless, similar to existing approaches, relying solely on
discrete pixel information for learning results in an unrealistic
depth distribution, leading to inaccurate predictions.
The proposed surface-continuous scene representation offers
a solution to address this problem. By incorporating the
planarity prior, the network can effectively learn continuous
surface signals. However, it is important to note that the plane
assumption does not always hold, leading to certain challenges
in FPRSO. Directly adapting the network to the sampled data
Rcont
img may result in sub-optimal outcomes, necessitating the
implementation of a suitable strategy for FPRSO.
Here, we propose a Planar Sampling Module that utilizes a
progressive learning strategy, as depicted in Fig. 4. We embed
three modules in backbone totally, respectively placed in front
of encoder and two Residual Blocks (predictor). Its input is
a combination Rdis
img of depth map and LF images/features,
and output is Rcont
img that consists of new depth map and LF
images/features. This module operates in three different modes
M, including: Zero, One and Multiple, (from easy to hard).
Zero. When M is set to Zero, the FPRSO is not performed
when data streams Dgt and l f pass through this module, which
is the simplest mode. Typically, PlaneNet adopts this mode
in early training phase to quickly narrow solution, and in
prediction for target inference.
One. When M is set to One, FPRSO is executed at most
once in a single forward (M will be update to Zero if FPRSO
is performed once). This allows backbone to use planarity prior
to eliminate some erroneous solutions during training, which
is the most basic use of FPRSO. We usually update M to One
after the Zero mode has basically converged during training
phase.
Multiple. After network converges in One mode, we use
the more complex Multiple mode to penalize backbone.
In this mode, FPRSO can be executed up to N times in a
single forward (N is the number of embedded modules, here
N = 3). We found that using Multiple in early stage of
training often makes network quickly converge to a suboptimal

In general, for planar sampling module, Zero is used in
early stage of training, One in middle stage, and Multiple
at last. Another, note that this module is parameter-free
and pluggable, this means it can be flexibly introduced into
most existing learning-based algorithms and improve their
performance. Experiments have proven this in Sec. V-E.
V. EXPERIMENT
In this section, we first introduce the implementation details,
and then compare our PlaneNet to several state-of-the-art
LF depth estimation methods in Sec. V-B. Furthermore,
we conduct additional experiments to further investigate the
effectiveness of our approach.
A. Implementation Details
1) Datasets: To validate the effectiveness of our proposed
method, we conduct experiments on both public and self-
rendered synthetic datasets: HCI 4D Light Field Dataset, Inria
Dense Light Field Dataset [40], UrbanLF [41] and real LFs
(Stanford Archives).
2) Training: Our PlaneNet is trained with a progressive
learning strategy. In first phase, we set M to Zero, (No
FPRSO is adopted). This phase lasts for five days until
backbone basically converges. Then in second phase, M is
updated to One, which lasts approximately One day. Finally,
Multiple is adopted and lasts for One day. Note that mode M
is automatically changed, and no manual operation is required.
During all training phases, we performed a large variety
of data augmentation, including random flipping and rotation,
brightness and contrast adjustment, noise injection, refocusing,
and downsampling. We use the Adam optimizer to minimize
the L1 loss, the batch size is 16 and the learning rate is 1e-3.
Our model is implemented in PyTorch and trained on Ubuntu
18.04.6 LTS with one NVIDIA Tesla V100. When testing,
we set M to Zero.
Additionally, note that the representation we proposed is
universal, and the designed FPRSO is pluggable, so more
versions
of
PlaneNet
can
be
explored
when
replacing
backbone, which is meaningful and interesting for LF depth
estimation. Therefore, we also design PlaneDistg/PlaneFocal
for experiments, which uses DistgDisp [24] / SubFocal [26]
as the backbone (Details can be found in Sec. V-E).
3) Evaluation: For performance evaluation, we use the
standard evaluation metrics in LF depth estimation, including
the mean square error (MSE×100) and bad pixel ratio
(BadPix(α)), represented by M and Bα respectively. Bα
measures the percentage of incorrectly estimated pixels whose
absolute errors exceeding a predefined threshold α.

B. Comparison With State-of-the-Arts
To prove the advancement of our method for LF depth
estimation, we compare PlaneNet with other state-of-the-art
methods.
1) Visual Comparison: Fig. 5 illustrates several examples

detail regions. It is evident that the results obtained by
our approach outperform the others. As an example, the
‘Origami’ scene exhibits intricate shapes and textures, while
the ‘Boxes’ and ‘Backgammon’ scenes involve occlusions
with depth discontinuity. In comparison to other methods,
our approach excels in preserving intricate details and
reconstructing regions with pronounced depth discontinuity.
Additionally, PlaneNet exhibits notable improvements in
achieving smoothness (‘Bedroom’), further highlighting the
effectiveness of our surface-continuous scene representation.

we perform ablation experiments related to the ‘Backbone

1) Surface Continuous Scene Representation: As described
in Sec. I, to address the issue of image information
discretization, we introduce a planarity prior to constructing
a surface-continuous scene representation and extend this rep-
resentation to the feature space. To validate its effectiveness,
we conduct the following experiments: 1) BaseNet: we set the
sampling mode to always be Zero (no representation applied),
making BaseNet the backbone of PlaneNet. 2) ImageNet:
we apply the proposed representation only to the input LF
images. 3) FeatureNet: the representation is applied only to the
feature level extracted by the network. 4) PlaneNet: PlaneNet
represents the complete model we propose.
As shown in Tab. V, PlaneNet achieves the lowest
mean squared error and bad pixel ratio compared to the
ablation models, validating the efficacy of our proposed
representation. BaseNet exhibits the worst performance among
the models, highlighting the utility of the surface-continuous
scene representation introduced in this work. When comparing
ImageNet, FeatureNet, and PlaneNet, we observe that applying
the representation at the feature level often yields superior
results
compared to
the
image
level,
underscoring
the
importance of incorporating FPRSO at the feature level.
Simultaneous application at both the feature and image levels
produces the best results, further demonstrating the versatility
and effectiveness of the proposed method.
2) Planar Sampling Module: As described in Sec. III,
to balance the ground truth with prior knowledge, we designed
and implemented a progressive sampling strategy in the planar
sampling module. To evaluate the impact of this strategy,
we designed three models: 1) BaseNet, 2) OneNet, and 3)
MultiNet, where the sampling mode is consistently set to
Zero, One, or Multiple during training. We present the
accuracy and convergence speed of the model under different
conditions in Tab. V and Fig. 6.
As shown in Fig. 6, methods using FPRSO consistently
achieve better results (lower validation MSE) than BaseNet,
demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed representation.
Upon examining the convergence curve, we observe that
applying a complex sampling mode M at the beginning often
leads to worse results but can accelerate model convergence.
For instance, OneNet converges slower than MultiNet but
achieves a lower MSE. This is because the complex sampling
mode M can lead to an overuse of the planarity prior,
causing the model to prematurely converge to a local optimal
solution. In contrast, our progressive sampling strategy starts
with the Zero mode, allowing PlaneNet to fully extract
depth information from the original dataset. Subsequently,
we employ FPRSO to iteratively refine the model in a step-
by-step manner (Zero-One-Multiple), effectively balancing
the dataset and prior knowledge, thereby achieving optimal
results.

E. Surface-Continuous Representation for Others
As described in Sec. III, the representation we proposed
can be seamlessly embedded into most existing LF depth
estimation methods due to the parameter-free, high efficiency
of FPRSO. Here, related experiments are carried out to prove.
1) PlaneDistg:
Similar to the structure of PlaneNet,
we choose DistgDisp [24] as the backbone network due to
its open source and efficient training, and embed the proposed
planar sampling module into network layers. Since the goal
is to verify the generalization of our representation, so we do
not explore the best combination.
As shown in Fig. 12, we follow wang [25] and divide
DistgDisp into four parts: feature extraction, cost construction,
cost aggregation, and depth regression. Since the feature map
becomes a 5D cost volume after cost construction, which is
somewhat different from the image representation, we only
insert the planar sampling module before and after feature
extraction. We convert the input LF images to the form of
sub-aperture images before entering it into planar sampling
module. For ease of introduction, we name this network
PlaneDistg.
2) PlaneFocal: We also choose SubFocal [26] as backbone
for a thorough experiment. Similar to PlaneDistg, we insert
the planar sampling module before and after feature extraction
module of SubFocal, then design the PlaneFocal.
When training PlaneDistg/PlaneFocal, the official weights
provided by wang [25] and chao [26] is taken as the initial
weights for the first stage, and we directly start the second and
third stages (One, Multiple) on this basis. Results are shown
in Fig. 12 and Tab. VIII, it can be found that by embedding
our representation, PlaneDistg and PlaneFocal significantly
exceeds the backbone network in various scenes.
VI. DISCUSSION
As discussed in Sec. I, we aim to mitigate the adverse effects
of discrete pixel data on LF depth estimation by introducing
a planarity prior to reconstruct a surface-continuous scene
representation.
To
integrate
this
representation
into
the
network, we designed the Plane Regular Sampling Operator
(PRSO) to digitize it. This digitized form is then incorporated
into various LF depth estimation networks through the
Planar Sampling Module. Extensive experiments demonstrate
improved performance when our representation is applied.
Additionally, the proposed network, PlaneNet, exhibits strong
generalization capabilities, effectively handling a wide range
of complex scenes.
However, our approach also has a notable limitation: in the
real world, curved surfaces are prevalent, and the assumption
of planarity can lead to estimation errors on such surfaces.
Although we have adopted several strategies to address this
issue, including (1) constructing planes between pixels rather
than object planes, which helps preserve the original image
information, and (2) employing a progressive learning strategy
in the planar sampling module to balance ground truth with
prior knowledge, the limitations of the planarity prior remain
unresolved in real-world scenes. Inspired by 2D Gaussian
Splatting [55] and PatchMatch [32], which compute a 2D plane
for each pixel, we plan to further refine the shortcomings of
our proposed approach in future work.
VII. CONCLUSION
In this paper, we propose a novel surface-continuous
scene representation using planarity prior for LF depth
estimation, which can overcome the discrete problems caused
by traditional image representation. We then present a novel
plane regular sampling operator and a progressive learning
strategy for plane modeling, resulting in convenient network
training. The designed PlaneNet can achieve competitive
results for LF depth estimation, 1st MSE ranking on HCI
benchmark. We also explored the application of FPRSO
at feature level and prove its effectiveness. Another, our
representation is universal and FPRSO is pluggable, which
has potential research significance and can bring insights to
this field.

papers.filename: TODE-Trans Transparent Object Depth Estimation with Transformer.pdf
papers.text:
TODE-Trans: Transparent Object Depth Estimation with Transformer
Kang Chen*1, Shaochen Wang*1, Beihao Xia2, Dongxu Li1, Zhen Kan1, and Bin Li1
Abstract— Transparent objects are widely used in industrial
automation and daily life. However, robust visual recognition
and perception of transparent objects have always been a major
challenge. Currently, most commercial-grade depth cameras
are still not good at sensing the surfaces of transparent
objects due to the refraction and reflection of light. In this
work, we present a transformer-based transparent object depth
estimation approach from a single RGB-D input. We observe
that the global characteristics of the transformer make it easier
to extract contextual information to perform depth estimation
of transparent areas. In addition, to better enhance the fine-
grained features, a feature fusion module (FFM) is designed to
assist coherent prediction. Our empirical evidence demonstrates
that our model delivers significant improvements in recent
popular datasets, e.g., 25% gain on RMSE and 21% gain on
REL compared to previous state-of-the-art convolutional-based
counterparts in ClearGrasp dataset. Extensive results show
that our transformer-based model enables better aggregation of
the object’s RGB and inaccurate depth information to obtain
a better depth representation. Our code and the pre-trained
model are available at https://github.com/yuchendoudou/TODE.
I. INTRODUCTION
A large volume of transparent objects such as glasses,
plastic bottles are frequently appearing in the household,
manufacturing, and daily life. The study of computer vision
on transparent objects has also attracted a great deal of
attention, including transparent area segmentation [1], [2],
3D reconstruction [3], especially for service robots [4] or
industrial transparent object manipulation [5]. Unfortunately,
transparent objects often lack their texture due to the re-
flection and refraction of light, which make them hard
to be distinguished from the background. The interactions
between visible light and transparent objects are complex and
sophisticated, and often hard to model. Generally, for many
household transparent objects, the bulk of visible light passes
directly through, and only a fraction (4% to 8%) [1], depend-
ing on the refractive indices, is reflected. This often results
in the background behind the transparent object dominating
the depth information of the objects. Estimating the depth
of transparent objects is critical for scene understanding, in
which the depth estimation of transparent areas allows us to
achieve a richer representation of the objects themselves and
surroundings. However, the existing commercial-grade depth
cameras, e.g., Intel RealSense [6], and Microsoft Kinect
* Contribute equally
1 University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, China
2 Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, China
Corresponding author: Zhen Kan (zkan@ustc.edu.cn)
This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foun-
dation of China under Grant 62173314, U2013601, U19B2044, 61836011,
and CAAI-Huawei MindSpore Open Fund.
Light refraction
Light
Depth camera
Reported  depth
Transparent object 
Actual depth
(a) Challenge 
(b) Potential application 
Fig. 1.
(a) shows the main challenges when estimating the depth
of transparent objects. The transparency of the object’s surface material
causes a large bias in depth prediction by the depth camera. (b) shows
some potential applications involving transparent objects such as object
manipulation and instrument processing.
[7], still can not provide satisfactory depth perception of
transparent objects. The reflection and refraction of light
around transparent objects lead to several challenges in the
transparent object image processing tasks. In scenes with
dense clutter of transparent objects, the edges of objects are
difficult to identify. In particular, in some extreme cases, a
few edges are even not visible at all.
Consequently, there have been many attempts to address
the depth estimation of transparent objects by deep neural
networks. Eigen et al. [8] are one of the first to use a
multi-scale convolutional neural network (CNN) to directly
predict the depth image from the color input. A ResNet-based
fully convolutional network architecture [9] is introduced
to regress the depth information. ClearGrasp [5] provides
a large-scale dataset of paired RGB-D transparent objects.
At present, a pretty intuitive approach is to train a fully-
convolutional neural network to directly regress the depth
information.
Besides, the CNN-based transparent object depth estima-
tion has a few limitations that the convolutional operation
is local and the restricted receptive field leads to poorly
extracted contextual information. Each convolutional kernel
concentrates more on a sub-region of the entire image. To
enlarge the receptive field, CNN-based models always keep
stacking convolutional layers, resulting in the loss of global
contextual information. Generally, transparent objects have
no color and their appearance is often heavily influenced
by background. Therefore, the model should have a stronger
capability to extract contextual information and retrieve cues
from the corresponding RGB and depth images. In fact,
2023 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2023)
May 29 - June 2, 2023. London, UK
979-8-3503-2365-8/23/$31.00 ©2023 IEEE
4880
2023 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) | 979-8-3503-2365-8/23/$31.00 ©2023 IEEE | DOI: 10.1109/ICRA48891.2023.10160537
Authorized licensed use limited to: University of Science & Technology of China. Downloaded on November 24,2023 at 07:39:42 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 
directly predicting the absolute depth from monocular images
is challenging even for humans. Therefore the model must
be strong enough in terms of contextual feature extraction.
In this paper, we gradually gather the token-based rep-
resentation from different transformer layers in the encoder
and progressively assemble them into a full-resolution depth
prediction. The decoder receives multi-scale representation at
different stages with global receptive fields. The fused feature
representation is eventually restored to a resolution of the
same size as the inputs for pixel-wise prediction. We conduct
extensive experiments on multiple datasets. The performance
of our model is improved by a large margin compared to pre-
vious best performing convolutional counterparts. Qualitative
results indicate that our model can produce more fine-grained
and globally coherent predictions.
The contribution of this paper can be summarized as
follows:
• To the best of our knowledge, this is one of the first
attempts to introduce a vision transformer to perform
depth estimation of transparent objects.
• TODE-Trans contains a contextual fusion module to
better perceive global information for further depth
prediction and shows promising generalization to novel
objects and unseen scenes.
• Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of
our model on a range of testing datasets. The studies
show that our model achieves competitive results com-
pared with existing solutions and also exhibits promis-
ing generalization across different datasets.
II. RELATED WORK
Transparent depth prediction and estimation. As the
depth sensing has received increasing attention over the
last decade due to its growing significance in autonomous
driving, 3D vision, and visual grasping. Earlier method [10]
used hand-crafted approaches to complete the missing values
on the surface of objects in the depth image. Several studies
usually assume that some information is already known, such
as background texture [11] or the 3D model of transparent
objects [12]. Previous work studies geometry-related proper-
ties and some patterns unique to transparent objects. Many
researchers [13], [14] are interested in studying the geometry
of transparent objects for reconstruction and composition.
Recent methods [8] model the depth sensing as a machine
learning prediction problem and use deep neural networks to
perform depth estimation. The benefit of using deep neural
networks for depth completion is that the model can learn
from data for depth predictions rather than copying and
interpolating from the input. Recently, the majority of current
works is still concerned with inaccurate depth estimation due
to LIDAR noise in outdoor conditions such as Cityscapes
[15] and KITTI [16] datasets. Even in indoor environments,
depth images captured by the RGB-D camera usually lose
more than half of the pixels on objects that are too far or too
close to the camera [17]. In addition, inferring the depth
of transparent objects in the scene not only suffers from
structural noise of the camera itself, but also from further
interference arising from refraction of light by transparent
materials. This problem is further exacerbated by refraction
on objects with too bright and glossy surface.
Currently, depth completion can be broadly categorized
into two classes according to the input type. The first class
directly regresses the depth map from the color image.
A number of approaches [18], [9], [19], [8], [20] adopt
convolutional neural networks to make deep predictions from
RGB images. Compared to the color-only input methods,
these approaches improve the performance but still suffer
from the generated low-quality results due to the limited
depth information. However, if the model’s predictions, e.g.
occlusion boundaries are inaccurate, it may lead to poor
results, and predicting multiple components would make the
optimization process very slow.
Transformer for dense prediction. Transformer [21] is
originally developed for sequence to sequence tasks, such as
machine translation, and question answering. Later, owing
to its excellent power to model the connections between
tokens in a sequence, the attention-based models, especially
transformer, have become the governing paradigm in nat-
ural language processing (NLP). ViT [22] has achieved a
further breakthrough by applying the standard transformer
from NLP to computer vision. A visual-oriented transformer,
Swin Transformer [23], is proposed that integrates both the
lone-range pixel modeling of attention mechanism and the
inductive bias of convolutional kernels for local information.
SETR [24] models semantic segmentation with transformer
encoder into a sequence-to sequence prediction task. Wang et
al. [25] present the use of transformer for visual grasping on
a physical robotic arm, and their results show that the global
features extracted by the transformer is more favorable for
dense pixel predictions. However, these mentioned works are
dedicated to outdoor scenes or other dense prediction tasks,
few of them concentrate on depth prediction for transparent
objects.
III. PROBLEM FORMULATION
Given a pair of the RGB scene image I ∈R3×H×W and
inaccurate depth image D ∈RH×W , the goal is to obtain
accurate depth predictions from monocular camera streams.
Our method entails generating a clear and complete depth
image ˆD ∈RH×W for transparent objects, where H and
W are the height and width of the input image. The entire
model is designed to learn a function f that maps the RGB
scene image I and inaccurate depth image D to a refined
depth prediction ˆD, defined in ˆD = f(I, D).
IV. METHOD
Architecture. As shown in Fig. 2, TODE-Trans contains
an encoder, a
fusion feature module, and a decoder. We
adopt the transformer in the encoder stage to globally ex-
tract features. A fusion perception module is included to
iteratively merge the captured features. We leverage swin
transformer block [23] as the backbone to achieve a nice
trade-off between local fine-grained features and global con-
textual features in the encoder phase. Our encoder consists

Fig. 2.
Overview of our proposed method. The model takes an RGB I ∈RH×W ×3 and an inaccurate depth map D ∈RH×W as input, and predicts
the depth output in an end-to-end manner. The entire network is composed of three stages: the first is a global feature extraction encoder consisting of
stacking transformer layers, the middle is a progressive depth refinement module, and the bottom is a decoder made of convolutional blocks that gradually
receive previous multi-scale features to recover the depth information of transparent objects.
of four stages. The first stage is composed of a patch
projection layer. Concretely, for an input RGB-D image,
we first split it into non-overlapping square patches with
patch size of 2, Then, the patches are treated as tokens. And
then they are flattened into vectors and fed into transformer
blocks. This kind of encoder allows us to develop multi-
scale features and can be used by the following module. The
attention mechanism globally learns the depth relationships
from different parts of the input image. We desire to conduct
the depth completion using more global information rather
than just local features from the color or raw depth image.
An overview of the swin transformer block is presented in
the bottom of Fig. 2. In the transformer block, at each layer,
a Layer Norm (LN) layer is first applied to the input feature
x before it enters the attention layer. The calculation flow is
shown as follows:

where LN refers to the layer normalization, and MLP means
a multi-layer perception with two layers. The feature xl−1 of
previous layer is first passed through window attention (W-
MSA) for local information attention calculation. Similarly,
then features are put through sliding window multi-head
attention (SW-MSA) to establish global feature correlations.
The foundation of the swin transformer block is the multi-
head attention. For the original swin-trainsformer, X is the
input to the multi-head attention. The input X is mapped to
the query, key, and value by linear transformation,
Q = XWQ, K = XWK, V = XWV ,
(2)
where WQ, WK, and WV refer to the learnable projection
matrices. Afterwards, the attention weights between features
are calculated by the inner product of query (K) and key (K),

In Fig. 2, we show that such global multi-scale feature
extraction contributes to the quality of the final depth predic-
tion, which naturally yields global coherent and fine-grained
prediction. Also, it becomes essential to further encode and
fuse the geometric features of transparent objects and its
surrounding spatial arrangements. Specially, a depth feature
fusion module is designed to enhance the information fusion,
taking the global geometric feature of transparent objects
from the encoder for further refinement. The entire FFM
consists of three feature fusion sub-modules [26] where each
sub-module improves the fusion efficiency by modelling the
correlation between feature channels. The sub-module in
FFM performs a global average pooling on the feature map
4882
Authorized licensed use limited to: University of Science & Technology of China. Downloaded on November 24,2023 at 07:39:42 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 
passed in from the previous layer in the encoder, scales
the generated output to the range [0,1], and multiplies this
value as scale with the feature map of the next layer in the
encoder. With this successive fusion operation, the fusion
sub-module enhances the important features and attenuates
the less important by controlling the magnitude of the scale,
thus making the extracted features more directional.
To reduce the number of parameters, a lightweight and
efficient decoder is constructed to obtain the estimated depth
map, which restores the features generated by 1× 1 convo-
lution kernel to a size of H × W. Similar to the encoder,
the decoder consists of four stages. Each stage contains
two convolution layers followed by one upsampling layer.
Except for the last stage, the output of the lower stage and
the corresponding output of our method together are used
as input. In fact, we found that the encoder incorporating
the attention as well as the decoder with convolutional
layers better can help the model in forecasting find-grained
information about the surface of transparent objects.
We formulate the transparent object depth estimation as a
dense regression problem. The object function is defined as:

where the first term is a reconstrction loss that minimizes the
L2 distance between the predicted depth ˆD and the ground
truth D∗. The second term is a regular term which penalizes
the inconsistency in the predicted depth. In addition, β is a
hyper-parameter that is set to 0.01. We train the model with
a weighted loss to supervised regression of the depth value,
Lfinal = αLmasked + βLunmasked,

where Lmasked denotes training by masking the non-

and Lunmasked indicates a pixel-wise training on the entire
input image. The final object function Lfinal can guide the
model to learn from color and raw depth images to produce
accurate and clear results.
V. EXPERIMENT
In this section, we perform a series of experiments to
evaluate our approach. Our investigation mainly concentrates
on the following questions: ”How does the model retrieve
clues for depth prediction from the colored image and
inaccurate raw depth image?”; and ”How well does the
model generalize across datasets?”.
A. Datasets and evaluation metrics
Dataset. The ClearGrasp [5] and Omniverse Object
dataset (OOD) [27] are used as the primary training and
evaluation datasets. i) ClearGrasp training samples are gen-
erated by Synthesis AI’s platform which is composed of nine
CAD models based on real-world transparent plastic objects.
The total number of the training dataset is 23524. For testing
samples, ClearGrasp consists of synthesis data and real-world
data. Each type of data all contains known data (i.e., the
transparent objects exist in the training set) and novel data
(i.e., the data is not contained by training dataset).
Fig. 3.
The samples of predicted depth images from our TODE-Trans
model. Under different lighting conditions and scenes, our model provides
effective depth prediction for transparent objects.
To access the generalization of the model, we also evaluate
the model on TransCG [28]. ii) TransCG utilizes a robot
to collect a new dataset based on real environments, which
contains 57715 RGB-D images captured by two different
cameras. The dataset contains 51 common objects in daily
life that may lead to inaccurate depth images, which include
reflective objects, transparent objects, translucent objects,
and objects with dense small holes.
Baselines. We use the RGBD-FCN proposed in [27] as the
baseline. The method directly uses Resnet34 as the network
structure to generate depth maps by direct regression. CG
[5] denotes the ClearGrasp algorithm, which adopts three
networks to infer the surface normals, masked transparent
surfaces, occlusion, and contact edge around transparent
surfaces. LIDF-Refine [27] is an abbreviation for the local
implicit depth function prediction followed by depth refine-
ment and is the current best algorithm on ClearGrasp dataset.
DFNet [28] is a lightweight variant of UNet.
Evaluation Metric. Following previous works [28], [27],
[17], we choose Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), Absolute
Relative Difference (REL), and Mean Absolute Error (MAE).

B. Implementation Details
All experiments are conducted on an Intel I5-12333 CPU
with 16 physical cores and an Nvidia RTX 3080Ti GPU.
For the training process, we set the batch size to 16. And
the resolution of images is set to 320 × 240 during training
and testing. In the process of training, we use the AdamW
optimizer to train our model with an initial learning rate
of 1e −3 and the weight decay of 0.05. A multi-step
learning rate schedule is utilized and the model is trained
for a total of 40 epochs. To prevent overfitting, a variety
of data augmentation methods is used before training, such
as random flipping, and rotating. Concerning loss, we set
β = 0.01. The embedding projection layer is implemented
by convolutional operator with a kernel size of 2 with stride
size 2. The final upsampling layer is followed by a 1 × 1
convolution to align the output to the desired depth image.
The model is implemented with MindSpore.

art approaches on the ClearGrasp dataset. The empirical

than other approaches on the real-known and real-novel

the same setting to guarantee a fair comparison. For CG
and LIDF-Refine, we use the released official codes and
default hyper-parameters for training. For other methods,
we present the results reported in their original papers. As
such, the methods that we consider for comparison include:
(a) the classical full convolutional neural network scheme,
(b) the current state-of-the art approaches in the transparent
depth completion, (c) the 3D vision-based depth estimation
of transparent objects, and (d) the convolution-based multi-
scale feature fusion scheme.
For our approach, we use swin-transformer as the back-
bone network to recover the exact depth image. We show the
point cloud built from our predicted depth image, from which
we can see that there is little difference between our results
and ground truth. For example, in Fig. 4, the 3D point cloud
of the transparent cups reconstructed from the original depth
sensors misses most of the details below the top of cups. On
the contrary, the whole transparent part is well reconstructed
and completed by the prediction of our transformer model.
We also tested our algorithm on TransCG, and the results
are shown in Table II. Except for the results of our model,
the results of the other algorithms are taken from the original
paper [28]. As shown in Tables I and II, our model achieves a
significant improvement on all metrics compared to previous
models.
D. Results Discussion
The low reflectivity of transparent materials can lead to in-
consistent appearance in different backgrounds, which causes
poor generalization of the model in novel environments.
We also conduct cross-domain experiments to show the
robustness and generalization of our method. Table III shows
the cross-domain experimental results for training validation
on different training and test sets. Similar to the experiments
in TransCG, we perform the following experiments: training
on ClearGrasp and OOD data and then testing on TransCG.
The results show that our method has higher robustness and
generalization than previous methods. In Table III, most of
these baselines are based on CNN and concentrate on local
features, looking for patterns in the neighborhood pixels.
Instead, the encoder in our model utilizes the attention
mechanism to capture relationships from different parts in
the input to recover the missing geometry.
First in the Table I, we compare the performance of
different models in different situations, such as real known
data, real novel data, etc. For instance, when trained on real

For example, in both case1 and case2, TODE-Trans generates a clear point

known data, the RMSE decreases from 0.028 to 0.021 with a
25% improvement compared to LIDF-Refine. Note that in all
cases our model is better than the full convolutional neural
network model (FCN). We conjecture that FCN suffers from
information missing in the encoder stage by progressively
downsampling during feature extraction so that the depth
can not be recovered well in the decoder process. Previous
methods such as FCN do not perform well, probably because
the convolution operators ignore the dependencies between
long-term features.
To help understand how the attention mechanism recovers
the geometric surface of transparent objects, we also visual-
ize the output of our model. In Fig. 3, we can observe that
our model maintains a clear boundary and refined detail.
The transparent object in the depth image often does not
provide sufficiently accurate depth data, partially due to the
reflection of structured light by the surface of transparent
objects. In Fig. 4, we can observe that more than one-third
of the pixel information in the transparent object area of the
raw depth image is missing. It is a great challenge for the
model to recover the depth information of the transparent
area from the rest of the raw depth image and the paired
RGB image, which demands a powerful ability to extract
contextual information.
E. Ablation Studies
In this section, to evaluate the quality of generated depth,
we utilize the original depth with missing and inaccurate
observations to complete the depth, and the ground truth with
RGB information of scene to render the surface reconstruc-
tion respectively. In Fig. 4, we also visualize the 3D point
clouds converted by RGB-D images for further comparison
and each row displays one example. Each pixel in the depth

image is rendered into a 3D point using camera intrinsics
provided by the dataset, where the color information is
obtained from the corresponding RGB input. The original
depth image captured by the sensor is missing largely. As
a result, the 3D geometry of point clouds reconstructed
using the raw depth has a lot of flaws and missing in
many places. In contrast, the surface reconstruction by our
predicted depth image contains less noise than the originals,
and the geometric appearance of the point cloud is much
clearer.
To investigate the effect of different modalities on the
depth estimation of transparent objects, we train the model
using RGB, depth, and RGB-D as inputs, respectively in
Table IV. In all of these, we adopt the same training
procedure and hyper-parameter setting as described earlier.
Experimental results reveal that simply considering RGB or
depth both ultimately degrades the final performance. Unlike
the conventional depth estimation, for transparent objects,
simply using RGB or depth image alone would hurt the final
predicted quality. Also, we can find that our model presents
a pretty sound balance in terms of the capacity and accuracy.
After investigating which input modalities are useful for the
transparent object depth completion, there are still many
interesting questions to be addressed such as whether the
fusion module is helpful for the depth prediction and how
the data augmentation contributes to the final improvements.
In Table I, we perform ablation experiments without the
fusion module and the models are all trained from scratch.
The qualitative and quantitative results demonstrate that the
fusion module contributes to the improved performance.
Intuitively, fusing information from the global context of
each stage is beneficial for the final depth prediction. From
these results, it is evident that our proposed method can
estimate the depth of transparent objects well.
VI. CONCLUSIONS
In this paper, we propose a model specially designed
for depth completion of transparent objects, which incorpo-
rates transformer and CNNs. Our model also follows the
encoder-decoder framework, in which the encoder leverages
the transformer to extract fine-grained feature representation
by modeling long-distance dependencies and global con-
text information. The decoder gradually aggregates features
from various semantic scales via skip-connections, and then
upsamples them to obtain the resulting depth estimation.
The experimental results show that our model with a trans-
former makes it easier to extract contextual information for
transparent object depth complementation, and shows good
generalization on cross-domain datasets.

Transfer learning-aided DETR framework for multi-view indoor infrared human
thermal adaptation behavior recognition and location
Wenrui Zhu
a, Junqi Yu
b,c,∗, Donghui Shi
d, Fangnan Donge, Ruifeng Huangf, Junmeng Songg
aSchool of Civil Engineering, Xi’an University of Architecture and Technology, No.13 Yanta Road, Xi’an, 710055, Shaanxi, China
bSchool of Building Services Science and Engineering, Xi’an University of Architecture and Technology, No.13 Yanta
Road, Xi’an, 710055, Shaanxi, China
cXAUAT Engineering Co.,ltd., No.13 Yanta Road, Xi’an, 710055, Shaanxi, China
dSchool of Electronics and Information Engineering, Anhui Jianzhu University, No.292 Ziyun Road, Hefei, 230601, Anhui, China
eSchool of Management, Xi’an University of Architecture and Technology, No.13 Yanta Road, Xi’an, 710055, Shaanxi, China
fHangzhou International Innovation Institute, Beihang University, No.166 Shuanghongqiao Road, Hangzhou, 311115, Zhejiang, China
gHefei Gocom Information Technology Co.,ltd., Hefei, 230061, Anhui, China
Abstract
Applying computer vision technology for human thermal adaptation behavior and location in the building indoor
environment enables real-time monitoring and feedback of thermal comfort levels. To overcome various challenges
associated with RGB-based methods, a transfer learning-aided detection Transformer framework (TLA-DETR) is
proposed for multi-view infrared indoor human thermal adaptation behavior recognition and location. This framework
effectively bridges the substantial modality gap between visible-light and infrared domains through a novel two-stage
transfer training paradigm. In the Transformer encoder/decoder, relative positional encoding is introduced to enhance
spatial awareness and the Fourier-modulated attention module is embedded to suppress noise. To make reliable
decisions from multi-view inputs, a fusion decision mechanism based on optimized Yager rule is designed to model
conflicting evidence. This work conducts multiple experiments and surveys within a commercial office building in
Xi’an, China. Results demonstrate that the TLA-DETR framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in thermal
adaptation behavior recognition and location.
Keywords: Human thermal adaptation behavior, Transfer learning, DETR, Infrared video understanding, Indoor
thermal comfort, Behavior recognition and location
1. Introduction
In modern society, global climate change and energy scarcity are increasingly acute challenges. Building energy
consumption constitutes one of the primary sources of global energy expenditure, accounting for approximately one-
third of total worldwide energy usage [1]. Some 40% of total building energy consumption is attributable to heating,
ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) systems [2]. Despite this substantial energy expenditure, many occupants
remain dissatisfied with the building’s indoor thermal environment.
With the intensification of urbanization, modern office workers engaged in commercial activities now spend over
90% of their daily time indoors. This makes the regulation of indoor thermal conditions a critical factor influencing
health and well-being [3]. Excessively high indoor temperatures or inadequate climate control can trigger various
health issues, such as cardiovascular and respiratory diseases [4, 5], all of which are closely associated with indoor
thermal comfort [6].
To ensure individual thermal comfort while simultaneously achieving energy conservation and emissions reduc-
tion, traditional constant-temperature setting strategies are gradually being replaced by occupant-centric adaptive
∗Corresponding author
Email addresses: wenruizhu_96@xauat.edu.cn (Wenrui Zhu
), junqi@xauat.edu.cn (Junqi Yu
)
control approaches [7]. Recent studies have emphasized the critical role of occupant behavior modeling in building
performance simulation and thermal comfort assessment [8, 9]. For instance, adaptive models based on field surveys
have successfully linked specific behaviors (e.g., window opening) to thermal sensation and energy use [10]. The core
principle of such methods is dynamically adjusting indoor environmental parameters based on real-time perception
of occupants’ actual thermal sensations. However, conventional intrusive or semi-intrusive approaches for acquiring
objective and subjective thermal sensation data exhibit inherent limitations in thermal comfort assessment [11]. In-
trusive approaches include questionnaire surveys, physiological signal measurements, and environmental monitoring.
Questionnaire surveys are highly intrusive, and the persistent, frequent requests for user feedback can disrupt partic-
ipants’ normal daily lives and work [12]. Physiological signal measurement requires sensors to be placed in direct
contact with human skin, with the sensation of a foreign object and restricted mobility being the primary obstacles to
such measurements [13]. The main challenges for environmental parameter sensors include spatial positional discrep-
ancies between the sensors and occupants, as well as the uneven distribution of indoor temperature, wind speed, and
illuminance levels [14, 15]. Semi-intrusive approaches primarily rely on wearable devices such as smart wristbands
[16, 17], glasses [18], or watches [19]. Compared to invasive measurements, semi-invasive measurements cause less
disruption to an individual’s behavior, though they still require the wearing of equipment.
Non-invasive approaches offer greater comfort and imperceptibility to occupants. Current non-invasive measure-
ments primarily utilize infrared (IR) [20–22] or visible-light (RGB) [23–25] cameras to capture temperature, heat
flux, or other physiological signals from the skin surface of exposed body regions, thereby assessing individual ther-
mal comfort. However, these approaches often overlook the influence of physical activity on physiological parameters
during non-static states, and individual subjective thermal perceptions may not always be reflected in measurable ob-
jective indicators.
Compared to temperature, heat flux, or other physiological signals, the instinctive thermal adaptation behaviors
exhibited by the human body reflect its adjustment to varying levels of thermal comfort from both objective and sub-
jective perspectives, which are more readily perceived and captured. Prior to visual approaches, extensive research
has explored sensor-based occupancy behavioral pattern recognition using ambient sensors. For instance, Dong and
Andrews [26] developed ambient sensor-based modelling using acoustics, CO2, and motion data to predict user be-
havior for energy and comfort management, demonstrating potential energy savings of 30%. Multi-sensor data fusion
approaches have further been applied to decode occupant behavior in shared spaces of open-plan offices by integrating
Wi-Fi and Bluetooth positioning with environmental data [27]. Additionally, sensor-fusion-based clustering analysis
has been used to reveal occupant behavior patterns in university building offices [28]. However, these non-visual meth-
ods often struggle to distinguish specific thermal adaptation behavior categories. Advances in computer vision have
enabled automated recognition of indoor human thermal adaptation behaviors, offering new possibilities for occupant-
centric building control. These studies primarily relied on RGB surveillance videos, employing skeleton extraction
or spatiotemporal feature models to classify behaviors [29–31]. Although thermal adaptation behavior recognition
methods based on indoor RGB surveillance videos have developed rapidly, two critical challenges remain unresolved.
First, the analysis of indoor RGB surveillance videos raises significant concerns regarding personal privacy [30]. To
address this, recent research has shifted toward non-intrusive thermal adaptation behavior recognition using Wi-Fi
channel state information (CSI) [32, 33]. While these approaches circumvent privacy-related controversies, their per-
formance is highly susceptible to degradation when Wi-Fi signals are subject to interference. Second, the data quality
of RGB videos is affected by ambient lighting conditions and illuminance levels within indoor environments [34].
Reflections of indoor light during daily hours and poor visibility caused by inadequate indoor light during night hours
can both degrade the data quality of RGB videos, thereby adversely affecting the performance of behavior recognition.
The adoption of indoor infrared surveillance videos can effectively deal with the two critical challenges mentioned
above. Infrared data primarily captures the thermal silhouette of the human body, rendering it virtually incapable of
discerning subtle facial differences. Compared to RGB data, infrared data is more privacy-friendly [14]. On the other
hand, the principle and mechanism of infrared thermal imaging inherently determine its insensitivity to indoor light-
ing and illuminance levels [35]. Nevertheless, existing research on thermal adaptation behavior recognition rarely
employs infrared data, owing to two intrinsic limitations associated with IR modalities. First, constrained by acqui-
sition costs, high-quality and well-annotated infrared video datasets are exceedingly scarce [36]. This necessitates
infrared human behavior recognition models to possess outstanding few-shot learning capabilities. Although exten-
sive indoor RGB surveillance video datasets are publicly available, the significant modality gap between visible-light
and infrared images renders direct transfer learning ineffective [37]. Second, infrared thermal imaging is prone to
2
noise interference caused by environmental factors and the imaging equipment itself [18]. While maintaining a stable
thermal environment is achievable through controlled HVAC systems to keep indoor temperatures constant, noise and
pixel-level distortions in infrared data generated by imaging equipment remain unavoidable. Mitigating the impact of
data noise presents a technical challenge for the robustness of thermal adaptation behavior recognition models.
Beyond the limitations inherent to the infrared data itself, related works on thermal adaptation behavior recognition
exhibit certain common limitations. For instance, scarcely any research has explored the spatial location of thermal
adaptation behaviors. In pattern recognition tasks, accurate location is as critical as classification. For thermal comfort
regulation in large indoor spaces, location information enables HVAC systems to determine the exact position where
activities occur, thereby providing the basis for accurate temperature and humidity control in localized zones. On
the other hand, a critical technical gap remains regarding multi-view visual information fusion in building operation
contexts. While numerous related works have reported collecting multi-view thermal adaptation behavior data from
indoor surveillance videos, none of them consider integrating multi-view information to resolve conflicts in detection
results between different views. In real-world indoor surveillance scenarios, infrared video data from multiple angles
often exhibit significant variations in thermal silhouettes due to view discrepancies. This leads to conflicting behavior
classification opinions and location bounding box predictions across views. Existing multi-source information fusion
decision-making strategies predominantly rely on traditional linear fusion decisions (majority voting or weighted
average sorting), which are based upon the assumption that evidence from each information source is independent
and equally reliable. However, in practical scenarios, significant discrepancies exist in the confidence levels and
spatial consistency across different views. Particularly when excessive conflicting predictions arise from multi-view
infrared data, simple linear fusion decisions yield a high probability of misclassification. Therefore, a unique fusion
decision mechanism for multi-view indoor infrared behavior recognition and location is required to explicitly model
uncertainty and appropriately handle multi-conflict evidence.
To address the aforementioned challenges and fill certain research gaps, this paper constructs a large-scale in-
frared dataset encompassing 26 categories of thermal adaptation behaviors in Section 2 (Data), and proposes a transfer
learning-aided DETR framework (TLA-DETR) in Section 3 (Methodology). TLA-DETR adopts a novel two-stage
transfer training paradigm, incorporates relative positional encoding and Fourier modulation attention (FMA) module
[38] within the Transformer encoder/decoder. Our latest work [39] explored preliminary efforts to apply multi-source
uncertainty fusion decision theory to skeleton-based action recognition tasks. Inspired by this work, this paper herein
designs a multi-view fusion decision mechanism based on Yager rules [40] specifically tailored for infrared human
thermal adaptation behavior recognition. In Section 4 (Experiments) and Appendix A/B, the behavior recognition and
location performance of TLA-DETR are objectively evaluated by multiple dimensions. These experimental dimen-
sions include sensitivity analysis of key hyperparameters, visualization result analysis, ablation study, decision case
analysis, robustness test, and comparative experiment. In Section 5 (Investigation and Discussion), further research
is conducted into the correlation between individuals’ subjective thermal sensations and their indoor thermal adap-
tation behavior patterns. It is important to clarify that the proposed behavior recognition framework is not intended
to replace established physical models like PMV, but rather to complement them by providing real-time behavioral
feedback that refines model inputs.
The specific contributions of this paper can be detailed as follows.
• constructs an indoor infrared thermal adaptation behavior dataset with 26 categories of thermal adaptation
behaviors (12 categories of heat-sensation-related behaviors and 14 categories of cold-sensation-related behav-
iors). To the best of our knowledge, this dataset represents the most comprehensive infrared dataset until now
for this task.
• proposes the TLA-DETR framework with two-stage transfer training paradigm to bridge the data modality gap,
achieving robust training performance with minimal infrared data samples.
• introduces relative positional encoding within the Transformer encoder/decoder to enhance the spatial aware-
ness of multi-head self-attention mechanisms and FMA modules within the TLA-DETR encoder/decoder to
suppress data noise.
• designs a multi-view fusion decision mechanism based on the optimized Yager rule. It performs twice iterative
fusion between three views’ classification opinions from the same infrared data sample, thereby enhancing the
accuracy and reliability of the final decision.
3
• TLA-DETR achieves state-of-the-art behavior recognition accuracy (94.2%) on the test set. Its behavior location
accuracy (88.1%) also surpasses most mainstream methods on the test set. Considering experimental results of
other dimensions, the improvements of TLA-DETR are both effective and reasonable.
• through a questionnaire survey conducted within a commercial office building in Xi’an, China, we investigate
the correlation between individuals’ subjective thermal sensations and their indoor thermal adaptation behavior
patterns. Random samples of the top-1 typical thermal adaptation behavior associated with 6 subjective thermal
sensation categories are chosen and tested by TLA-DETR. The results further validated the model’s reliability.
2. Data
2.1. Related Datasets
Based on thermal adaptation behavior datasets from multiple related works [29, 30, 41–43], a summary of various
human behaviors associated with heat sensations and cold sensations is presented in Table 1. Here, B001–B012 denote
numbers of heat-sensation-related behaviors, while B013–B026 denote numbers of cold-sensation-related behaviors.
The symbol "✓" indicates that the dataset includes the corresponding behavior category. Among all relevant studies,
the dataset constructed by Duan et al. [29] contains the most thermal adaptation behavior categories, totalling 16.
In this paper, we propose a new Thermal Adaptation Behavior Dataset (TABD), which extends these 16 behaviors
by adding 4 additional heat-sensation-related behaviors (Swing hair, Stretch limbs, Take off gloves, and Pull away
the collar) and 6 cold-sensation-related behaviors (Pull the collar tight, Put on gloves, Put hands in pockets, Cross
one’s legs, Ring hands around neck, and Shivering). TABD encompasses a total of 26 behavior categories, covering
all behaviors listed in the aforementioned referenced datasets and significantly increasing the challenge of thermal
adaptation behavior recognition tasks.
The PMV (Predicted Mean Vote) index is an indicator used to quantify thermal sensation within the thermal
comfort prediction model proposed by Fanger et al. [44]. Based on the human heat balance equation, the PMV index
integrates four physical variables: air temperature, air velocity, mean radiant temperature, and relative humidity. It
categorizes indoor thermal sensation into seven levels: cold (-3), cool (-2), slightly cool (-1), neutral (0), slightly warm
(+1), warm (+2), and hot (+3). The American Society of Heating, Refrigerating, and Air-Conditioning Engineers
(ASHRAE) has adopted the PMV index in Standard 55 [45] to guide the calculation and assessment of thermal
comfort. Concurrently, ISO 7730 [46] recommends maintaining the PMV index within the range of 0 ± 0.5 as the
ideal thermal comfort level. Building upon prior research by Duan et al. [29], we further establish correlations
between 26 thermal adaptation behaviors and the PMV index, as presented in Table 1.
2.2. Data Collection Details
The infrared data in TABD are collected from the real-world indoor environment of a commercial office building
in Xi’an, China. Different levels of thermal environments (both heat and cold) are simulated by precisely controlling
the temperature, airflow, and humidity of the HVAC system, as detailed in Table 2. The participants include 4 work-
place volunteers in an indoor office environment. To ensure the representativeness of the sample while minimizing
confounding factors from inter-individual differences, we carefully select participants with quantified demographic
and physiological characteristics, as summarized in Table 2a. Specifically, the participant group comprises 2 males
and 2 females, with ages ranging from 24 to 38 years (mean: 31.0 ± 6.2 years), body mass indices(BMI) within the
normal range of 18.9–24.3 kg/m² (mean: 21.8 ± 2.3 kg/m²), and self-reported metabolic rates consistent with light
office work (1.0–1.2 met). All participants are non-smokers, have no known thermoregulatory disorders, and report
similar thermal preference patterns in preliminary screening questionnaires. Participants wear standardized office at-
tire (cotton shirt, trousers/skirt, and light shoes) with clothing insulation values constrained within 0.5–0.7 clo. This
range represents typical summer office clothing and reduces thermal signature variability due to garment material or
layering differences. Before each data collection session, participants are explicitly informed of the required move-
ments and angles. To fully simulate indoor thermal adaptation behaviors, all behavioral recordings are conducted
and collected under expert supervision to ensure consistent execution in terms of movement amplitude, duration, and
repetition frequency. Each participant completes simulations of all 26 behavior categories, with 150 shots (450 raw
video clips with 3–8 seconds in duration) per category.

To capture multi-view infrared data, three Hikvision dual-spectrum barrel cameras (HM-TD2668-25/T1 [47]) are
deployed to record from three distinct angles: front view, lateral view, and back view. Each camera is equipped with
an uncooled vanadium oxide (VOx) microbolometer thermal sensor, operating within the long-wave infrared (LWIR)
spectral band of 8–14 µm with a 25 mm focal length and NETD < 30mK (25°, F1.0). The resulting thermal video
resolution is 640×512 pixels, recorded at a frame rate of 25 fps. The cameras are positioned 1.8 m from the subjects,
mounted at a fixed height of 1.5 m with a 15° upward angle.

2.3. Data Preprocessing and Partitioning
First, we performed preprocessing on the collected raw video data. This step involved removing duplicate and
degraded video clips, downsampling longer clips, and interpolating frames for shorter clips. Ultimately, all infrared
video clips are standardized into MP4 format, with a frame rate of 15 fps and a duration of approximately 5 seconds.
The TABD dataset comprises a total of 11,308 thermal behavior video clips, including 5,275 clips associated with
heat-sensation-related behaviors and 6,033 clips associated with cold-sensation-related behaviors. The inter-category
variation in the number of samples remains within 5%. Sample frames for all 26 thermal adaptation behaviors in
TABD are shown in Figure 1.
To prevent data leakage and ensure methodological rigor, the dataset is partitioned into training, validation, and
test sets at the video clip level using a group-aware strategy. Specifically, all three views(front, side, and back) of a
single video clip are kept together in the same set(i.e., they are never split across training, validation, or test sets). This
ensures that the model is evaluated on unseen video events rather than unseen views of known events. The partition
