3. Methodology
TLA-DETR employs a two-stage cross-domain transfer learning framework, aiming to address the challenge of
efficient detection and location of indoor thermal adaptation behaviors under infrared data scarcity (few-shot learning
conditions). Its core principle involves pre-training a robust feature extraction and detection architecture on large-scale
visible-light datasets, then transferring visual information to the infrared domain through a unique transfer learning
trick (freezing the CNN backbone + parameter sharing of the Transformer encoder/decoder + fine-tuning only the
FFNs) to achieve efficient domain adaptation.
3.1. Overall Architecture
The overall architecture of the proposed TLA-DETR is illustrated in Figure 2. This approach employs DETR as
its baseline framework and comprises three key components: a feature extractor, an object encoder/decoder, and a
prediction head.
The feature extractor comprises a CNN backbone, a 1x1 convolutional layer, and positional encoding. First, the
input raw image (3 × H × W) is fed into a CNN backbone (ResNet-50 [48]), which maps it to a high-dimensional
feature map (2048 × H/32 × W/32). This feature map is subsequently reduced in dimension via a 1x1 convolutional
7
layer to yield a low-dimensional, high-semantic input representation (256 × H/32 × W/32). To encode spatial con-
text, automatically generated and dimensionally aligned learnable positional encodings are element-wise added to the
feature map, thereby embedding explicit positional information for each feature vector.
The feature map dimensions (H/32 × W/32 × 256) are reshaped for input into the Transformer encoder, yielding an
output feature map of identical size (encoder output feature). N object queries are constructed with 256-dimensional
learnable embedding vectors as prior information for the Transformer decoder.
The prediction head comprises multiple parallel, non-weight-sharing Feed-Forward Neural Networks (FFNs), each
responsible for mapping the output features of the Transformer decoder to the target classifications (N × classnum)
and position regressions (N×4). The regression results encompass the normalized coordinates, width, and height of
the predicted bounding box.
3.2. Cross-domain Transfer Learning
After introducing the key components of TLA-DETR, Figure 2 also visually illustrates the process of transfer
learning-assisted model training. This process consists of two stages: the source domain pre-training stage and the
target domain transfer training stage. In stage 1, the model undergoes end-to-end training on large-scale visible-light
video clips (source domain). These annotated RGB data are mainly derived from multiple related thermal adaptation
behavior datasets referenced in Section 2.1, including works by Duan et al. [29] (70% training data of 14,800 video
clips for 16 behaviors), Liu et al. [30] (64% training data of 27,504 video clips for 13 behaviors), Li et al. [41]
(70% training data of 840 video clips for 7 behaviors), and some RGB video clips that we collected as supplementary
data. By adopting this mixed approach to construct the source domain dataset, the aim is to balance the RGB sample
accounts for different behavioral categories. After supplementation, the source domain dataset comprises a total of
54,236 video clips. The sample account for each behavior is approximately 6 times that of the corresponding category
in TABD training set. The objective of this stage is to enable the entire network to learn general and robust visual
representation capabilities. When the model transitions to training on few-shot infrared video clips (target domain) in
stage 2, the following modifications must be made to define the label mapping and weight transfer strategy.
• Freeze CNN Backbone: The weights of the CNN backbone (ResNet-50) for front-end feature extraction in
Stage 1 are loaded and completely frozen (requires grad=False) during Stage 2. This measure preserves low-
level visual features (edges, contours) that are modality-invariant, and prevents catastrophic forgetting caused
by excessive discrepancies in the density distribution of data information when extracting infrared features.
• Share Encoder/Decoder Parameters: The weights of all parameters of the object encoder/decoder layer trained
during the pre-training stage are fully preserved and directly transferred as the ‘backbone network’ to the target
domain. These deep Transformer architectures have maximally learned complex visual contexts and global
dependencies from RGB features after pre-training. The parameter-sharing setup significantly enhances the
efficiency of transfer training on infrared data.
• Fine-tune FFNs: While keeping the deep neural network layers unchanged, only the prediction head of the
model undergoes fine-tuning using only a limited amount of infrared data. The FFN weights for classification
and bounding box regression require reinitialization and are subjected to end-to-end supervised learning on the
infrared training set. Specifically, FFNclass adopts cross-entropy loss to learn the unique thermal signatures
of each object within infrared images, while FFNob ject adopts L1/L2 loss on infrared data to accurately fit the
bounding boxes of infrared targets. The prediction head undergoes partial weight migration based on label
overlap. For classification head (FFNclass), the corresponding weights of 26 behavior categories are directly
loaded from Stage 1. For regression head (FFNob ject), all bounding box regression weights are reinitialized
randomly and learned solely from the infrared training set (object scales and distributions differ between RGB
and IR domains).
During Stage 2, the AdamW optimizer is configured to update only the parameters of the Prediction Head (FFNs).
The optimal learning rate setting for the initialized weights is shown in Appendix A.

3.3. Encoder/Decoder Design of TLA-DETR
One of the core challenges in indoor thermal adaptation behavior recognition lies in the fact that behavior patterns
depend on spatial relationships with long-range temporal sequences (e.g., "pull away the collar—take off clothes").
Compared to traditional CNN-based models, Transformer-based architectures (e.g., DETR [49] and its variants) are
better suited for modeling these global, long-distance semantic dependencies. Concurrently, behavior recognition
via infrared imaging also faces challenges like ambiguous target (human) morphology (lack of geometric texture)
and complex backgrounds (interference from thermal sources and noise). For these, we specifically design the en-
coder/decoder architecture of TLA-DETR, as shown in Figure 3 (a). The encoder/decoder innovatively adopts a
multi-head self-attention module (MHA) enhanced with relative positional encoding to learn geometric priors, thereby
overcoming the limitations of absolute positional encoding in infrared human behavior recognition. Simultaneously,
a Fourier Modulation Attention (FMA) [38] module is introduced to actively suppress noisy frequency components
and enhance the learning of global thermal radiation distribution patterns in infrared images.
The encoder of TLA-DETR consists of N identical encoder layers stacked sequentially. Each layer receives the
feature map (H × W × C) extracted from the front-end CNN, which is first flattened into a sequence form (HW ×
C) and added with relative positional encoding to preserve two-dimensional spatial information. Subsequently, this
sequence is fed into the MHA and FMA modules to capture global contexts at low computational cost. Finally, the
output is mapped through the FFN module (typically composed of two linear layers with GELU activation function)
into a set of image feature representations rich in global semantics and spatial relationships for use by the decoder. It is
worth noting that each module is sequentially processed through residual connections (Add) and layer normalization
(Norm) to prevent degradation in deep layers.
The decoder of TLA-DETR also consists of M identical decoder layers stacked sequentially. Its input comprises
two components: first, the image feature representations from the encoder; second, learnable object queries, the
number of which equals the predefined maximum number of detectable objects. Each object query represents a
candidate representation of a potential target, with its initial value being a randomly initialized vector. First, the object
queries still undergo feature extraction via the MHA and FMA modules, subsequently concatenating these with image
features output by the encoder to dynamically focus on the regions within the image most relevant to the current
9
query. The fused features are similarly processed through the M-layer iterative decoding process to produce a set of
refined object queries. Each refined query is finally fed into the prediction head (comprising a classification head and
a bounding box regression head) to generate the final behavior recognition and location results.
3.3.1. MHA Module Enhanced with Relative Position Encoding
In infrared images, the thermal silhouette of the human body is the sole cue for behavior recognition. While
lacking the texture and color present in visible light, its spatial geometric relationships are highly stable. For instance,
the head, torso, and limbs of a person always exhibit specific relative positional distributions in infrared thermal
imaging, while behaviors like raising hands or stomping feet alter the relative distances and angles among these body
parts. The sinusoidal spatial positional encoding employed by standard Transformers is absolute (based on pixel
coordinates within the image), yet in indoor environments, the position of human behaviors within the image may
undergo significant displacement. The model requires relative relationships rather than absolute coordinates. Relative
positional encoding enables the model to learn invariant geometric priors such as "the head is always approximately 20
pixels above the torso" or "the hands are typically located on either side of the torso". This significantly enhances the
model’s robustness to pose variations and positional displacement. When the model detects one “heat plaque” directly
above another, it can immediately leverage prior knowledge encoded by relative positional encoding to determine
that this configuration is more likely to represent a “person” rather than “other heat sources”. This geometry-based
learnable relative relationship modelling is the key to distinguishing subtle behaviors in infrared images.
The usage of relative position encoding for attention weight computation constitutes a refined enhancement of
the conventional MHA mechanism. This module is designed to explicitly introduce relative spatial positional priors
while preserving the multi-head architecture (enabling parallel computation of multiple attention heads), thereby
overcoming the deficiency in geometric perception caused by relying solely on absolute position encoding in standard
attention mechanisms. Its embedding process is illustrated in Figure 3 (b). First, the feature sequence is linearly
projected into three tensors: query (Q), key (K), and value (V). The projection matrices are implemented as 1 × 1
convolutions (WQ, WK, WV), maintaining consistent projection dimensions (H × W × C). Relative positional encoding
encodes the relative two-dimensional offset between two pixels (xi, xj) via an embedding table, generating a relative
positional embedding matrix r of the same dimensionality as Q and K. During attention weight computation, the
original standard dot product aij between query and key is extended as follows.

3.3.2. FMA Module
Infrared thermal imaging fundamentally represents a thermal radiation map of temperature distribution, with its
information primarily manifested in the distribution of low-frequency and mid-frequency energy (e.g., the human ther-
mal silhouette appears as a smooth, continuous low-frequency region), while high-frequency components are predom-
inantly noise (e.g., sensor noise, minor environmental temperature fluctuations). The multi-head attention mechanism
in the original DETR operates at the pixel level and is highly sensitive to high-frequency noise in infrared images.
This leads to a dispersion of attention weights, preventing the model from focusing on the holistic thermal silhou-
ette of the human body. To address this, TLA-DETR employs the FMA module to refocus on human contours amid
10
high-frequency noise. The FMA module separates noise (high-frequency) from human signals (low-frequency/mid-
frequency) via spectral-domain computation. Compared to computing pixel displacements, subtle movements exhibit
more pronounced energy changes in the spectral domain. Through learnable modulation functions, FMA can actively
suppress noise frequency components while enhancing frequency components that represent human contours and key
body parts (e.g., head, torso), thereby obtaining semantic representations of the critical features of behaviors. On the
other hand, thermal signatures of human behavior exhibit distinct periodicity and spatial distribution patterns. For
example, a "waving hand" motion generates a thermal trail moving across space, while “stomping feet” produces a
rapidly moving, low-intensity heat source. These patterns manifest in the spectral domain as specific frequency signa-
tures. The FMA module enables the model to directly learn and recognize these global thermal distribution patterns
without requiring pixel-by-pixel tracking in the pixel domain. This significantly enhances recognition capability for
complex, long-term behavior patterns while reducing computational complexity on high-resolution infrared images.
As illustrated in Figure 3(c), the FMA module pairs real fast Fourier transform (RealFFT) with 1 × 1 convolution
to project spatial features into the spectral domain for learning frequency information. The spectral-domain features
are subsequently reprojected back into the spatial domain via inverse fast Fourier transform (IRealFFT). According
to the spectral convolution theorem, this operation enables contextual learning from local to global scales along both
spatial and channel dimensions.
Specifically, the input feature X is first processed via layer normalization. The normalized feature is then trans-
formed from the spatial domain to the spectral domain via 2D discrete RealFFT. The transformed spectral-domain
feature ¯A is further processed via 1 × 1 convolution to implement spatial encoding of frequencies within the spectral
domain, aiming to enable effective manipulation of the entire image-level spectrum and capture the global receptive
field.

3.4. Multi-view Fusion Decision Mechanism
In complex indoor surveillance scenarios, single-view video data is limited by occlusion, pose variation, and
view deviation. This often leads to low classification confidence or inaccurate bounding box location for behavior
recognition models. For example, actions captured from front and back views exhibit significant differences. The side
view, although capable of capturing action amplitude, is prone to background interference that causes bounding box
drift. Most decision mechanisms for multi-view behavior recognition are based upon the assumption that evidence
from each information source is independent and equally reliable. However, in practice, this assumption is invalid
under most conditions. When multiple views yield conflicting predictions (e.g., the front view predicts "putting on
clothes", while the side view predicts "taking off clothes"), a mathematical tool is required to handle such evidence

conflicts to aid decision-making. The Dempster-Shafer evidence theory [50] is precisely a generalized probabilistic
reasoning framework designed for such issues. It overcomes the limitation of classical Bayesian probability that
requires uncertain mass to be assigned to specific events, allowing partial belief to be allocated to the entire set Θ (i.e.,
the uncertain state), thereby more accurately reflecting ambiguity and incompleteness in decision-making. Based upon
this theory, we propose an Optimized Yager rule to integrate behavior classification and localization opinions from
multiple views (View1, View2, View3). This rule represents a fusion decision mechanism for uncertainty reasoning
specifically designed to handle multi-view conflicts.
This mechanism models the behavior classification confidence and the IoU between the prediction bounding box
(bbox) and ground truth (gt) output by TLA-DETR for each view’s data, as two distinct types of uncertainty evidence.
It then performs uncertainty modeling via basic probability assignment (BPA). The specific workflow is illustrated in
Figure 4.
First, classification evidence modeling is required. For the i-th (i = 1, 2, 3) view, the model outputs a behavior
prediction category ci and its confidence score conf ci ∈[0,1]. Define the identification frame Θc= ω1, ω2, ..., ωn
(n=26) as the set of all possible behavior categories. The classification BPA is constructed as follows.
m (ci) = conf ci,
u (ci) = m (Θc) = 1 −conf ci,

where this allocation indicates that the direct belief degree of prediction category ci is conf ci. The remaining 1 −

frame Θc (i.e., ’uncertain which class’). A denotes any other subset of Θc, and its BPA is 0.
Second, location evidence modeling is required. To evaluate the spatial reliability of the prediction bounding box,
computing each view’s IoUi between bboxi and gti is necessary. Similarly, define the location frame Θb as the set
of all possible positional hypotheses (typically using cluster centroids or average boxes as candidates). The location

In indoor environments, evidence derived from multi-view infrared data is more likely to be conflicting. The
classical Dempster combination rule would normalize the conflict to Θ when handling conflict evidence, leading to
unreliable results. The Yager combination rule [40] directly allocates the conflict to Θ, which better aligns with the
intuition that "conflict equates to uncertainty". Therefore, we adopt the optimised Yager rule to replace the classical
Dempster rule for multi-source information uncertainty fusion decision.
The standard Yager combination rule applies only to the fusion of two evidence sources. For two branch opinions
(X, Y) and their corresponding BPA (ma, mb), the joint belief mass (m(X), m(Y)) and the joint uncertainty (u) computed
via the Yager rule are as follows.

4. Experiments
4.1. Implementation Details
The experiments for TLA-DETR involve a two-stage training process. As shown in Figure 2, the model is first
training in Stage 1 (pre-training in source domain) with large-scale visible images, then training in Stage 2 (transfer
training in target domain) with annotated infrared training set of TABD. Sensitivity analysis of key hyperparameters
is conducted based on action recognition accuracy on the test set of TABD (the validation set of TABD is solely used
to verify whether transfer learning affects experimental result), ultimately determining the experimental performance
under the optimal parameter configuration.
13
Figure 5: Confusion matrix of the TLA-DETR’s recognition results for 26 thermal adaptation behaviors.
All the experiments are implemented in the PyTorch framework (python 3.11.1, torch 2.3.0, torchversion 0.18.0)
under the Ubuntu 22.04 LTS system on an Nvidia 3090 GPU with CUDA version 12.1 and cuDNN version 8.9.7.
The total training epochs for the entire training phase is capped at 120. The AdamW optimizer is employed with the
weight decay of 0.0001, the batch size of 16, and the momentum of 0.9. Both the number of encoder layers (N) and
decoder layers (M) for TLA-DETR are set to 6. The number of object queries is set to 100, and the dimension of
positional encoding is set to 256. Based on the optimal test results reported in Appendix A, we ultimately set the
number of training epochs in Stage 1 to 80 and in Stage 2 to 40. The initial learning rate of Stage 1 is set to 1e-2,
while Stage 2 requires fine-tuning FFNs with its learning rate adjusted to 1e-3. When performing multi-view fusion
decision via optimized Yager rule, the weight coefficient α is set to 0.6.
4.2. Visualization of experimental results
The recognition results of TLA-DETR for 26 thermal adaptation behaviors are shown in the confusion matrix
in Figure 5. This matrix clearly illustrates the model’s classification performance on each individual category, with
diagonal values representing the percentage of correctly classified instances for each behavior. Among all behavior
categories, the model achieves its highest score for B017 (Put hands in pockets), reaching an accuracy of 99.77%. In
total, 21 behaviors achieve recognition accuracy exceeding 90%, of which 12 behaviors surpass 95%. These results
demonstrate the outstanding performance of TLA-DETR in indoor infrared thermal adaptation behavior recognition
tasks.

values being closely aligned. This indicates that the model not only correctly predicts most behavior categories but
also does not exhibit significant tendency toward either missed detections or false positives. Test results exhibit certain
discrepancies across different behavior categories, which are likely primarily attributable to the ambiguity of similar
behaviors within infrared data.
The bar chart in Figure 6(d) illustrates the average IoU (Avg IoU) between bbox and gt for each behavior category,
which serves as the core metric for evaluating location accuracy. Compared to the aforementioned metrics, test results
of Avg IoU are more balanced, ranging from [87.1%, 89.6%], with top-5 scores being 89.6% (B020), 89.4% (B012),
89.2% (B007), 88.9% (B005), and 88.8% (B023), with an average value of 88.10%. This result indicates that the
bbox predicted by TLA-DETR exhibits high overlap with gt, demonstrating accurate location with minimal influence
from different behavior categories.
In addition to the above visualization results categorized by individual behaviors, Table 3 also presents the overall
recognition accuracy and overall location accuracy for behavior categories classified according to heat/cold sensation-
related attributes. It is worth noting that due to slight imbalances in sample amount per behavior category (as described
in Section 2.3), the overall recognition accuracy does not equal the average value of Recall among all behavior cate-
gories, and the overall location accuracy does not equal the average value of Avg IoU among all behavior categories.
These two overall metrics are calculated via weighted averages from independent assessments of each sample in-
stance in the test set. Specifically, the overall recognition accuracy is calculated by dividing the number of correctly
classified samples in the test set by the total number of samples in the test set. Consequently, it reflects the model’s
overall performance under the real data distribution, and behavior category with larger sample amounts exert greater
influence on the final result. Data in this table indicates that the overall recognition accuracy for cold sensation-related
behaviors (95.7%) exceeds that for heat sensation-related behaviors (92.3%). This advantage is primarily due to cold
sensation-related behaviors containing more training and test samples, enabling the model to learn their patterns more
thoroughly. Comprehensively, the overall recognition accuracy across all 26 behavior categories reaches 94.2%. Sim-
ilarly, the overall location accuracy is calculated by computing the IoU between each bbox and its corresponding gt
box within the test set, then averaging the IoU values across all samples. This approach ensures that each data sample
contributes equally to the final location performance evaluation, avoiding bias introduced by disparities in category
sample amounts. The results indicate that, whether evaluating heat sensation-related behaviors, cold sensation-related
behaviors, or all behavior categories combined, the model’s overall location accuracy remains within the range of
88.1%-88.2%.
16
4.3. Cross-Subject Generalization Test
While the primary experiments in Section 4.2 validate the model’s performance under a standard clip-level data
partition (where all participants are represented in training and test sets), a critical question remains regarding the
model’s ability to generalize to unseen individuals. In real-world building deployments, the system must accurately
recognize behaviors from occupants who are not part of the initial training dataset. To address this concern and evalu-
ate the robustness of TLA-DETR against inter-individual differences (e.g., body shape, clothing insulation, metabolic
rates, and behavioral habits), we conduct an additional cross-subject generalization test using a Leave-One-Subject-
Out (LOSO) protocol.
In this LOSO setup, the dataset is partitioned based on participant identity rather than video clips. Specifically, for
each iteration, data from three participants are used for training and validation, while data from the remaining held-out
participant was reserved exclusively for testing. This process is repeated four times, ensuring that each participant
serves as the test subject exactly once. It is important to note that the group-aware clip-level split strategy described
in Section 2.3 is still enforced within each fold to prevent multi-view data leakage.
The results of the cross-subject generalization test are summarized in Table 4. The average recognition accuracy
across the four folds is 89.5%, with a standard deviation of 1.8%. The average location accuracy (Avg IoU) is
87.8%, with a standard deviation of 0.2%. This degradation is expected and primarily attributable to the domain shift
caused by individual physiological and behavioral variations that were not present in the training set. For instance,
participants with higher body mass indices may exhibit different thermal radiation patterns, and gender differences
may lead to distinct behavioral preferences for the same thermal sensation.
Despite this decline, the LOSO results demonstrate that TLA-DETR retains a high level of reliability (nearly
90% accuracy) even when deployed on unseen occupants. This confirms that the two-stage transfer learning strategy
successfully learns generalizable visual features from the source RGB domain that are robust to individual differ-
ences. For building engineering applications, this level of generalization is sufficient for initial deployment, where the
model can provide immediate zone-level control feedback. Furthermore, this result highlights the potential value of
future online learning mechanisms, where the model could be fine-tuned incrementally as new occupant data becomes
available in the specific building environment.

4.4. Ablation Study
To systematically validate the effectiveness of each core component within the TLA-DETR model, we conduct
a detailed ablation study. This involves investigating the impact of the transfer learning strategy, the FMA module,
relative positional encoding, and the multi-view fusion decision mechanism on behavior recognition and location
performance. All experiments are performed under identical training/test set partitions.

First, it is necessary to validate the proposed two-stage cross-domain transfer learning framework. The ablation
study setup and results for this part are presented in Table 5. A single-stage DETR model trained and tested on the
TABD is chosen as the baseline. Due to the limited sample amount of infrared training data, the baseline achieves
moderate performance in both behavior recognition accuracy (79.3%) and location accuracy (84.8%). Compared
to the baseline, TLA-DETR with two-stage classical transfer learning strategy (freezing the CNN backbone + fine-
tuning only the FFNs) significantly improves behavior recognition and location performance to 92.4% (↑13.1%) and
87.5% (↑2.7%), respectively. This fully demonstrates the importance of pre-training in data-scarce tasks. Based upon
standard transfer, all parameters of the encoder/decoder are further shared. TLA-DETR with this complete transfer
strategy achieves optimal performance, demonstrating that parameter sharing of deep Transformer architecture enables
the complete transfer of complex visual context and global dependencies learned in the source domain to the target
domain, thereby facilitating optimal domain adaptation.
Next, it is required to investigate the contribution of the FMA module in handling infrared noise and extracting
global thermal patterns. The ablation study setup and results for this part are presented in Table 6. We revert the FMA
module in the encoder/decoder of TLA-DETR back to the standard MHA module in conventional Transformer archi-
tecture while keeping all other settings unchanged. This variant model achieves a behavior recognition accuracy of
90.6% and a location accuracy of 87.9%. After introducing the FMA module, model performance improves to 94.2%
(↑3.6%) and 88.1% (↑0.2%). This result indicates that, compared to behavior location, the FMA module places
greater emphasis on improving behavior recognition performance by suppressing high-frequency noise in infrared
data.
Then, the impact of relative positional encoding and its embedding position on model performance needs to
be evaluated. The ablation study setup and results for this part are presented in Table 7. If the relative positional
encoding in the encoder/decoder of TLA-DETR is reverted back to the standard cosine absolute positional encoding
in conventional Transformer architecture, although recognition accuracy remains largely unchanged (93.5%, ↓0.7%),
location accuracy significantly declines to 82.0% (↓6.1%). This indicates that absolute encoding is overly sensitive
to the precise spatial position of objects. When human positions shift within indoor environments, it causes the
prediction bounding box to drift, thereby reducing the behavior location accuracy. If relative positional encoding
is added solely to the input of the Transformer encoder, the location accuracy recovers to 86.6%, yet it still lags

Finally, comparing the impact of different information sources (multi-views) and decision mechanisms on the
behavior recognition and location performance is essential. The ablation study setup and results for this part are
presented in Table 8. According to this table, both information sources and decision mechanisms significantly affect
behavior recognition outcomes. As the benchmark, we first evaluate the behavior recognition performance using a
single view only. When making decisions based on any one of the three views, the frontal view (86.0%) and back
view (81.5%) perform relatively better, while the side view performs worst (68.9%). This result lags behind most
other multi-source information fusion decision mechanisms, reflecting the inherent limitations of relying on a single
view. If a simple majority voting strategy is applied across three views’ predictions with the highest vote category as
the final decision, its behavior recognition accuracy (83.8%) is even lower than that achieved by the single front-view
decision. This indicates that when multiple conflicting evidence exists, this strategy has a low probability of producing
correct decisions. If prediction results are weighted average sorting according to classification confidence with the
optimal result (highest-ranking) selected as the final decision, the behavior recognition performance can be improved
to 89.3%. This proves that incorporating confidence as weights is more effective than majority voting in integrating
multi-branch opinions. When applying the classical Dempster-Shafer rule for three-view fusion decision, the behavior
recognition accuracy can be improved to 91.5%. The optimized Yager rule proposed in this paper directly quantifies
conflict as uncertainty, achieving optimal recognition performance. Notably, further analysis of all two-view fusion
decisions for behavior recognition reveals that the Yager rule consistently outperforms the Dempster-Shafer rule.
This conclusion also fully exposes the limitations of the classical Dempster-Shafer rule, which is that normalization
can lead to counterintuitive results when handling multi-opinions with high-uncertainty evidence. The Yager rule
avoids forcibly assigning conflicts to any specific category, making it more robust when facing multi-view conflicts
in complex scenarios. Unlike behavior recognition, behavior location results are unaffected by the fusion decision
mechanism and are primarily constrained by the information source. For single-view input, the overall behavior
location accuracy equals the arithmetic mean of Avg IoU across all samples. The TABD stipulates that multi-view
19
data for each behavior sample is strictly partitioned at a ratio of 1:1:1. Therefore, for multi-view input, the overall
behavior location accuracy equals the arithmetic mean of multiple single-view location accuracies. Taking all results
with three-view input as examples, their behavior location accuracy remains consistently at 88.1%, which is the
arithmetic mean of the three single-view localization accuracies (91.7%, 82.4%, 90.2%). This conclusion also aligns
with the behavior localization uncertainty modeling described in Section 3.4.

4.5. Robustness Test
To comprehensively evaluate the stability and reliability of the TLA-DETR in real, complex indoor environments,
this section establishes a robustness test. This test is designed to simulate various interference and degradation factors
that may be encountered in indoor surveillance scenarios, including but not limited to influences from the environment,
equipment, and the test subjects (humans) themselves on infrared imaging. As illustrated in Figure 7, we introduce
six distinct perturbation factors into each original video sample of the test set. This perturbation factors include mirror
flip, random scaling, random occlusion, Gaussian noise, salt-and-pepper noise, and Poisson noise.
The specific configuration details are described as follows.
1. Mirror Flipping: The input video clips are horizontally flipped to simulate the impact of different human motion
directions on model testing.
2. Random Occlusion: Within the human bounding box, black squares of random size (covering 10%–50% of the
human region) are applied to simulate partial occlusion of the body by objects or other individuals.
3. Random Scaling: The human target (defined by its bounding box) is randomly scaled to 50%–150% of its
original size and then padded back to the original video dimensions. This is common in surveillance scenarios (where
subjects approach or recede), enabling assessment of the model’s adaptability to variations in target size.
4. Gaussian Noise: Adding Gaussian noise with a mean of 0 and variance randomly selected within the range of
[0.005, 0.02] to the original video to simulate electronic sensor noise during the sensor camera capture process.
5. Salt-and-Pepper Noise: Pixels in the original video are randomly set to pure black (0) or pure white (255) with a
probability randomly within the range of [0.005, 0.02] to simulate sporadic errors during infrared signal transmission.
6. Poisson Noise: Adding Poisson-distributed noise with a signal-to-noise ratio randomly selected within the
range of [15dB, 35dB] to the original video. New pixels are generated via random Poisson sampling to simulate
photon discretization noise in infrared imaging.
Table 9 compares the performance degradation of TLA-DETR under various perturbations. The data in this ta-
ble indicates that minor perturbations (mirror flipping and Gaussian/salt-and-pepper noise) exert the least impact on
model performance. Specifically, mirror flipping has almost no effect on location accuracy, while Gaussian and salt-
and-pepper noise are effectively suppressed by the FMA module in the spectral domain. Consequently, both recog-
nition and location accuracies decline below 2%. Moderate perturbations (random scaling and Poisson noise) cause
noticeable yet controllable decline in performance. With random scaling, behavior recognition accuracy declines to
91.8%. Yet, owing to the relative positional encoding, behavior location accuracy reaches 88.5%, even surpassing
the initial location accuracy without any perturbation. It is speculated that the resampling operation during the scale
transformation process filters out some sharp edge artifacts and noise through smoothing, thereby rendering the hu-
man thermal silhouette clearer and more regular, which facilitates more precise location by the model. Poisson noise
is the most realistic noise source in infrared imaging. Introducing Poisson noise with random signal-to-noise ratios in
the test set reduces behavior recognition accuracy to 91.1%, yet this remains significantly superior to most state-of-
the-art (SOTA) methods in Section 4.5. Severe perturbation (random occlusion) is the greatest challenge, as it directly
disrupts the human thermal silhouette. Nevertheless, TLA-DETR still achieves a behavior recognition accuracy of

4.6. Comparison with SOTA Methods
To comprehensively evaluate the performance of TLA-DETR on indoor thermal adaptation behavior tasks, we
conduct a systematic comparison with mainstream state-of-the-art infrared behavior recognition and video under-
standing methods in recent years. All methods are trained and tested on TABD (using the group-aware clip-level
split described in section 2.3), with results shown in Table 10. According to the type of backbone network, these
methods can be categorized into CNN-based methods (TSN [51], Deep-IRTarget [52], MDJ+TPN [53], YOFIR [54],
and MFCF-Net [55]), Transformer-based methods (DETR [49] , UniFormerV2 [56] , ViTDet [57], MViTv2 [58],
Hiera [59], RT-DETRv2 [60], and CAGTDet [61]), and hybrid methods (FEN+SCA [62], Video-FocalNets [63], and
MobileNetV2-CA-MHA-GRU1 [34]). Some CNN-based and hybrid methods focus solely on behavior recognition.
Since they lack object detection capabilities, their location accuracy cannot be calculated. Their performance can only
be measured via recognition accuracy.
Constrained by architectural bottlenecks, CNN-based methods generally exhibit mediocre performance, and prove
inadequate when handling the characteristics of infrared data, such as their lack of texture and high noise levels.
YOFIR (2025) [54] represents the state-of-the-art CNN baseline specifically optimized for the infrared domain. By im-
proving the YOLO architecture, YOFIR achieves the highest location accuracy of 90.2%, demonstrating its formidable
real-time object detection capability. However, it treats object detection and behavior classification as two separate
tasks, heavily emphasizing on location performance while neglecting optimization for behavior recognition. Conse-
quently, its action recognition accuracy (87.8%) lags behind the state-of-the-art end-to-end Transformer-based method
(CAGTDet [61]). Benefiting from the advantage of self-attention mechanisms in modeling global relationships, the
average performance of Transformer architectures surpasses that of CNN architectures in the same era. For example,
UniFormerV2 (2022) [56], ViTDet (2022) [57], and MViTv2 (2022) [58] achieve both higher behavior recognition
and localization accuracies than Deep-IRTarget (2022) [52]. It is noteworthy that CAGTDet (2024) [61] achieves a
high recognition accuracy of 92.5% by introducing context-aware gating mechanism, second only to the performance

model, MobileNetV2-CA-MHA-GRU1 (2025) [34] achieves outstanding recognition performance on its originally
reported 11 thermal adaptation behaviors. Yet, it still does not implement behavior location functionality. Its test
accuracy on the 26-category similar and easily confused behavior recognition of TABD is 91.8%, still impressive
but lags slightly behind CAGTDet [61] and TLA-DETR. It is speculated that such complex serial architectures may
impede gradient propagation, and GRUs may exhibit forgetting when processing long-sequence video clips.
The visualization scatter plots in Figure 8 (a) and (b) distinguish all methods by their backbone architectural cat-
egory through color and shape, separately compare recognition and location performance for heat sensation-related
behaviors (B001–B012) and cold sensation-related behaviors (B013–B026). Heat sensation-related behaviors typi-
cally involve rapid, large-scale limb movements and significant heat dissipation, resulting in dramatic thermal silhou-
ette variations and numerous easily confused actions. In contrast, cold sensation-related behaviors are predominantly
slow, small-amplitude movements that lead to the accumulation of heat sources. These two distinct physical processes
impose different challenge requirements on models. Compared to heat sensation-related behaviors, nearly all models
exhibit improved performance on cold sensation-related behaviors. In terms of the magnitude of improvement, certain
CNN-based methods show the most pronounced gains, likely because the more concentrated and stable heat sources
of cold sensation-related behaviors are visually easier captured by CNNs’ local receptive fields. The performance
fluctuation range (3.4%) of TLA-DETR across two distinct types is moderate compared to other Transformer-based
methods. Most notably, TLA-DETR occupies the farthest right position in both subgraphs, indicating its absolute
superiority in behavior classification capability. Simultaneously, TLA-DETR consistently occupies a relatively up-
per region of each subgraph, signifying that its behavior location accuracy also ranks among the top tier. This fully
demonstrates that the improvements proposed in this work are not specialized optimizations for any particular behav-
ior category, but fundamentally address a common challenge in infrared behavior recognition–namely, how to extract
stable and reliable spatiotemporal semantic information from ambiguous and noisy thermal imaging. Analyzing per-
formance distribution differences across various behaviors not only fails to diminish TLA-DETR’s leading position
but further validates its robust vitality as a general-purpose solution.
5. Investigation and Disscusion
Beyond the technical performance metrics of Section 4, the results of this work offer several practical implications
for building engineers and facility managers. First, the achieved recognition accuracy of 94.2% suggests that the
TLA-DETR framework is sufficiently robust for deployment in real-time HVAC control loops. Second, the location
capability (88.1% IoU) enables zone-level control strategies, allowing building management systems to adjust tem-
perature setpoints only in occupied zones exhibiting thermal stress, rather than conditioning the entire space. Finally,
the non-intrusive nature of infrared sensing addresses the privacy barriers that have historically limited the adoption

of camera-based occupant monitoring in commercial buildings. By integrating this framework with existing Build-
ing Management Systems (BMS), facility managers can transition from static schedule-based control to dynamic,
behavior-driven optimization, potentially reducing HVAC energy waste while improving occupant comfort. The il-
lustrative engineering simulation in Appendix D also confirms this point.
Considering that human thermal comfort is significantly influenced by individual variation, in addition to provid-
ing the objective correlation between the thermal adaptation behaviors and the PMV index in Table 1 of Section 2.1,
our work further explores the relationship between these behaviors and subjective thermal sensations through survey
interviews. Within a commercial office building in Xi’an, China, we collect a total of 635 valid survey questionnaires.
The questionnaire contains six targeted single-choice questions (details in the supplementary material S 1), which can
be broadly categorized into two types of questions concerning heat/cold sensation. The framework for the first type of
three questions is as follows: "In the office, when you feel hot/warm/slightly warm, which of the following behaviors
do you most habitually and instinctively perform?". The options for these questions include 12 heat sensation-related
behaviors (B001–B012). Similarly, the framework for the second type of three questions is as follows: "In the of-
fice, when you feel slightly cool/cool/cold, which of the following behaviors do you most habitually and instinctively
perform?". The options for these questions include 14 cold sensation-related behaviors (B013–B026).

By aggregating the voting results from the survey questionnaires, we statistically analyzed the common behaviors
with the top-5 voting rate and their corresponding proportions under six distinct subjective thermal sensations, as
illustrated in Figure 9. When subjective sensation reports feeling hot or warm, individuals tend to adopt cooling
measures. Among these, B009 (Take off coat) and B005 (Roll up sleeves) emerge as the most prevalent choices. This
indicates that increasing skin exposure area to promote convective heat exchange and utilizing sweat evaporation to
dissipate heat constitute primary behavioral adaptation strategies under conditions of high-level thermal discomfort.
When subjective sensation reports feeling slightly warm or slightly cool, behavioral choices became more diverse.
As these states approximate approach the most comfortable natural condition, individuals rarely resort to drastic heat
dissipation or insulation measures. Instead, they tend towards tentative adjustments through relatively conservative
and minor behaviors like B011 (Take off gloves) and B017 (Put hands in pockets). When subjective sensation reports
feeling cool or cold, individuals will demonstrably shift towards behavioral patterns aimed at keeping warm, including
but not limited to reducing skin exposure area to minimize heat loss like B016 (Put on clothes) and B022 (Rub hands),
or generating heat through vigorous activities like B026 (Shivering) and B025 (Stamp one’s feet). These survey
findings not only reflect the potential correlation between specific behaviors and subjective thermal sensations, but
also provide valuable data support for future research.
For the Top-1 typical thermal adaptation behaviors corresponding to the above six subjective thermal sensation
states, we further employ TLA-DETR to conduct random sample inspections. The corresponding behavior recog-
nition and location results are illustrated in Figure 10. Beyond demonstrating TLA-DETR’s single-view behavior
recognition and location capability under different subjective thermal sensation states, these inspection results also
profoundly reveal its advantages in multi-view conflict decision-making. Among these randomly sampled cases, mul-
tiple instances of multi-view conflicts are evident. For example, both B009 and B026 exhibit correct opinions for the
front view and side view but incorrect opinions for the back view. For B016, the opinions of front view and back view
are correct while the opinion of side view is incorrect. B011 exhibits the most severe conflict, with only the opinion
of front view being correct while both the opinions of side view and back view are incorrect. Despite such conflicting
opinions, TLA-DETR still makes correct classification decisions for every typical thermal adaptation behavior cate-
25
gory, unequivocally reaffirming the reliability of the proposed multi-view fusion decision mechanism. Additionally,
the red bbox for each behavior exhibits high overlap with the green gt box, achieving an Avg IoU range of [0.875,
0.890]. The overall location offset of target behaviors is minimal and closely conforms to the human thermal silhou-
ette. For certain fine-grained behaviors dominated by specific body parts, such as B005 and B011, the bbox tightly
aligns with the boundaries of the torso and arm regions executing these behaviors.
While the questionnaire survey establishes a qualitative correlation between behaviors and subjective sensations,
a critical question remains: how can recognized behaviors be quantitatively translated into thermal comfort indices?
We posit that the proposed TLA-DETR framework serves as a complementary module to the standard PMV model,
rather than a replacement. The traditional PMV model, defined by Fanger [44], calculates thermal sensation based on
six primary variables.
PMV = f

Ta, ¯Tr, va, RH, M, Icl

(15)
where Ta is the air temperature (°C), ¯Tr is the mean radiant temperature (°C), va is the relative air velocity (m/s), RH
is the relative humidity (%), M is the metabolic rate (met), and Icl is the clothing insulation (clo). In conventional
building management systems, M and Icl are often treated as static inputs or estimated based on fixed schedules, which
fails to capture dynamic occupant adjustments.
To bridge this gap, the recognized thermal adaptation behaviors can be used to correct these input parameters
dynamically. Based on ASHRAE Standard 55 [45] and ISO 7730 [46], specific behaviors correspond to quantifiable
changes in metabolic activity and clothing ensemble. For illustrative purposes, the behavior of putting on clothes
(e.g., B016) typically implies an increase in clothing insulation. According to standard clothing ensembles, adding
a jacket or sweater corresponds to an approximate increase of 0.3 to 0.5 clo. Conversely, taking off clothes (e.g.,
B009) suggests a reduction of similar magnitude. Similarly, active behaviors like shivering (B026) or stamping feet
(B025) indicate a transient increase in metabolic rate beyond the baseline sedentary level of 1.0 met, potentially adding
approximately 0.2 to 0.4 met depending on intensity.
By integrating the real-time behavior classification outputs from TLA-DETR, the PMV calculation can be updated
as follows.
PMVadjusted = f

Ta, ¯Tr, v, RH, Mbase + ∆Mbehavior, Icl,base + ∆Icl,behavior

(16)
where ∆Mbehavior and ∆Icl,behavior are correction terms derived from the probability distribution of detected behaviors.
This approach allows the physical model to account for the occupant’s active thermal regulation strategies, thereby
improving the accuracy of thermal sensation estimates without discarding the physical rigor of the PMV framework.
Therefore, this work analogizes behavior recognition to a non-intrusive sensor layer, which can enhance rather than
replace the existing thermal comfort evaluation standards.
While the proposed TLA-DETR framework demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on the TABD dataset, sev-
eral limitations regarding generalizability must be acknowledged to contextualize these results for broader building
engineering applications. First, the dataset is collected from only four participants within a single commercial office
building under controlled HVAC conditions. Although this controlled setup ensures data quality for algorithm vali-
dation, it may not fully capture the diversity of real-world occupant populations. Inter-individual differences, such as
variations in body shape, metabolic rates, clothing insulation habits, and cultural behavioral norms, could influence the
thermal signatures captured by infrared cameras. For instance, individuals with higher body mass indices may exhibit
different thermal radiation patterns compared to slender individuals, potentially affecting the model’s feature extrac-
tion efficiency. Similarly, cultural differences in thermal adaptation behaviors (e.g., preference for wearing layers
vs. adjusting thermostats) may introduce domain shifts when deploying the model in different geographical regions.
Second, environmental diversity remains a constraint. The current study is conducted in a specific office environment
with consistent background thermal properties. In realistic deployments, dynamic background heat sources (e.g., di-
rect sunlight, operating equipment, or varying wall temperatures) could introduce additional noise that differs from the
controlled training conditions. While the Fourier Modulated Attention (FMA) module enhances robustness against
standard noise, extreme environmental variations may still lead to performance degradation. Therefore, the claimed
state-of-the-art performance should be interpreted within the boundary conditions of this study. The TLA-DETR
framework is validated primarily for office-like environments with similar thermal settings. The detailed analysis
of these performance limitations and boundary conditions is presented in Appendix E. To address these limitations,
26
future work will focus on expanding the dataset to include diverse participant demographics and multiple building
environments.
6. Conclusion
To address the core requirements for intelligent detection of human thermal comfort in indoor building environ-
ments, this work constructs TABD, proposes and validates TLA-DETR – a framework for identifying and locating
human thermal adaptation behaviors based on infrared data. The establishment of TABD fills the gap in high-quality
annotated data for the field of infrared thermal adaptation behavior recognition. TLA-DETR achieves high-accuracy
recognition and location of 26-category thermal adaptation behaviors (12 heat sensation-related behaviors and 14 cold
sensation-related behaviors). This model performs efficient and stable training on extremely limited infrared data,
fully demonstrating the potential of cross-domain feature transfer in few-shot learning. According to the characteris-
tics of infrared data, multiple key optimization components are incorporated into the encoder/decoder to effectively
enhance the model’s spatio-temporal feature extraction and anti-interference capabilities. To address the uncertainty
arising from multi-view conflicts, TLA-DETR abandons conventional linear fusion decision mechanisms, instead pro-
poses and adopts a unique multi-view fusion decision mechanism based on multi-source information fusion theory
to enhance the credibility of the decision-making process. A series of experiments conducted on TABD validate the
superiority of TLA-DETR across multiple dimensions. Correlation analysis with survey results further corroborates
the feasibility of employing thermal adaptation behaviors as proxy variables to infer subjective thermal perception.
Future implementation should focus on integrating this behavioral feedback loop with standard PMV calculations,
where recognized behaviors dynamically adjust model parameters to achieve higher assessment accuracy.
The current validation is based on a limited participant group and a single building context. We will consider
individual differences and environmental diversity to ensure robust performance across varied building scenarios,
and strive to explore broader applications of TLA-DETR within intelligent building environmental control systems.
Meanwhile, integrating infrared data with other sensor data to construct a more comprehensive human thermal comfort
assessment framework, aiming to thoroughly quantify subjective human thermal sensation via objective evaluation
results.

Appendix A. Sensitivity Analysis of Key Hyperparameters
In deep learning-related experiments, the configuration of key hyperparameters can significantly influence the
results. To ensure fairness, while keeping all other settings unchanged as specified in Section 4.1, we conduct a
quantitative sensitivity analysis on three hyperparameters specific to this work (typical values derived from grid search
results). These three key hyperparameters comprise the learning rate η of FFNs in Stage 2, the ratio β of training
epochs between two stages, and the weight coefficient α between classification and location evidences.
η governs the rate at which the detection head (FFNs) of the model updates its weights during transfer training
stage on the target domain. As mentioned in Section 3.2, during this stage, the parameters of CNN backbone are
frozen, the parameters of Transformer encoder/decoder are shared, and only the parameters of FFNs for classification
and regression are fine-tuned. Therefore, η is the sole optimizer hyperparameter requiring adjustment that directly
affects network parameter updates. If the learning rate is too large, the fine-tuning process may cause significant dis-
ruption to the powerful, generalizable feature representations learned by the pre-training stage on the source domain,
leading to catastrophic forgetting. If the learning rate is too small, the weight updates of FFNs will proceed extremely
slowly. Furthermore, as the TABD training set for Stage 2 constitutes few-shot data, the model may be unable to fully
adapt to the unique thermal radiation features of infrared data within a limited number of training epochs, leading to
underfitting. The ideal value of η requires maintaining an optimal balance between "preserving source-domain knowl-
edge" and "adapting to target-domain characteristics". Table A.11 presents the test accuracy of behavior recognition

exhibits a trend of initially rising before subsequently declining. The highest test accuracy (94.2%) is achieved at
η=1e-3, and this setting is adopted as the optimal learning rate for fine-tuning FFNs in all subsequent experiments.
β represents the resource allocation ratio for two-stage training within the overall training epoch limit (120 epochs).
This ratio (Stage 1 epochs : Stage 2 epochs) determines how many iterative learning steps each stage can perform.
Figure A.11 illustrates the training loss curves and test accuracy curves under four typical values of β (0.2, 0.5, 1,
2), and the specific stable values and convergence speed are listed in Table A.12. To more intuitively demonstrate
the effect of transfer learning, we also employ the training loss curve and test accuracy curve of DETR (without
transfer-learning strategy) as a baseline. Comparative results indicate that, regardless of the value of β, the final stable
loss value of TLA-DETR (Stage 2) on the training set is consistently lower than that of DETR (without transfer
learning strategy), and the final stable accuracy value of TLA-DETR on the test set is consistently higher than that
of DETR (without transfer learning strategy). This confirms the effectiveness of the transfer learning strategy. As
shown in Figure A.11(a), (b), and (c), as β increases, the training loss decreases while the test accuracy increases,
and both convergence speeds improve. This phenomenon indicates that within a certain range, more training epochs
in Stage 1 on the source domain, the better training and testing performance in Stage 2 on the target domain. Upon
reaching a certain threshold (as shown in Figure A.11(d), where training loss for Stage 1 converges around 72nd
epoch), the training loss stabilizes at approximately 0.16 without further decrease, and the test accuracy stabilizes at
approximately 94.2% without further improvement. Ultimately, this setting (β=2) is adopted as the optimal ratio of
training epochs between two stages in all subsequent experiments.
α is a hyperparameter that balance the relative importance between classification evidence and location evidence
at the decision level. Table A.13 presents the test accuracy and overall average uncertainty (uc
final) under six typical
values of α (0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1). As α approaches to 0, the decision favors reliance on the IoU between bbox and gt.
However, conflicts between prediction bounding boxes from different views are unavoidable (for instance, prediction
bounding boxes from side view may drift due to background interference), rendering judgments based solely on
location evidence unreliable. As α approaches to 1, the decision favors reliance on the classification confidence of each
view. Yet, under severe occlusion or interference, relying solely on classification evidence may neglect the corrective
role provided by spatial location, leading to unreliable judgments. A reliable decision should simultaneously consider
two dimensions: "how confident the model’s prediction about the behavior classification is" and "how accurate the
model’s estimation about the behavior location is". Searching for the optimal value of α on the test set essentially
explores how to most effectively combine these two complementary types of evidential information, thereby enabling

To more clearly illustrate the multi-view fusion decision-making process based on the optimized Yager combina-

detailed steps of fusion decision are shown in Figure B.12.

view opinion B is B004 (mc = 0.36, mb = 0.81), and the back view opinion C is B003 (mc = 0.45, mb = 0.89).

Appendix C. Survey Questionnaires about Subjective Thermal Sensation
See Supplementary Material (S1).pdf.
Appendix D. Illustrative Engineering Simulation of Behavior-Driven HVAC Zoning Control
To demonstrate the practical implications of the proposed TLA-DETR framework for building operation and
translate TLA-DETR’s recognition outputs into measurable operational benefits relative to a baseline control strategy,
this appendix presents an illustrative engineering simulation focusing on zone-level HVAC control based on localized
cold-related behavior detection. A one-week field pilot study was conducted in the same commercial office building
where the TABD dataset was collected. This study compares the theoretical projections with actual field test results
(as shown in Table D.16) to evaluate the practical feasibility and performance gaps of the behavior-driven HVAC
control strategy.
Scenario and Requirement: A 1000 m2 indoor office space divided into 10 thermal zones, each monitored by
independent infrared camera nodes (a thermal zone requires three infrared cameras with different views, an edge
computing device like NVIDIA Jetson AGX for real-time inference, and a BACnet or Modbus protocol interface for
communication with existing BMS) running the TLA-DETR model. Each zone accommodates approximately 1-2
occupants during standard operating hours (8:00-18:00). For the field pilot study, two adjacent zones (Zone A and
Zone B) with similar envelope characteristics were selected for detailed comparison.
Baseline Strategy (Zone A): A conventional rule-based control maintaining a constant heating setpoint of Tbase =
22◦C across all zones, regardless of occupant behavior. This represents the standard practice in many commercial
offices and serves as the reference for benefit comparison.
31
Table D.16: Comparison between Theoretical Projections and Actual Field Test Results

Note: Projections are based on TLA-DETR performance metrics (Section 4.2) and standard HVAC engineering coefficients
(ASHRAE). Theoretical PES (1.5%) is calculated via Eq. D.3; actual PES (1.2%) is measured via smart meters. CIR is
represented by PPD reduction. Field test results are averaged over 5 working days in March, 2025.
Behavior-Driven Strategy (Zone B): A dynamic control strategy utilizing TLA-DETR outputs. The core objec-
tive is to dynamically adjust the temperature setpoint (Tset) of specific zones based on the aggregated thermal stress
indicators derived from behavior recognition. Unlike traditional thermostat-based control that relies solely on air
temperature, this behavior-driven approach incorporates occupant responses as direct feedback. The control logic is
defined as follows.
For each thermal zone z, the system counts the number of occupants (Nzone) exhibiting cold-related behaviors
within a sliding time window ∆t (e.g., 5 minutes). A Zone Cold Stress Index (CS Iz) is calculated to quantify the
severity of thermal discomfort.
CS Iz(t) =
PNzone
i=1
P
k∈Ωcold wk · Ii,k(t)
Nzone
(D.1)
where CS Iz directly drives the setpoint adjustments shown in the "Avg. Heating Setpoint" row of Table D.16. Ωcold
represents the set of cold-related behavior categories, Ii,k(t) is an indicator function (1 if occupant i performs behavior
k at time t, else 0), and wk is a weight factor based on the severity of the behavior, derived from the PMV correlation
in Table 1. The temperature setpoint for zone z is adjusted according to the following threshold-based rule.
Tset ,z(t + 1) =

Tset ,z(t) + ∆T,
if CS Iz(t) > θhigh
Tset ,z(t),
if θlow ≤CS Iz(t) ≤θhigh
Tset ,z(t) −∆T,
if CS Iz(t) < θlow
(D.2)
where ∆T = 1.0◦C is the adjustment step, and θhigh = 0.3, θlow = 0.1 are calibrated thresholds (e.g., θhigh = 0.3
indicates > 30% of occupants showing cold signs). In the theoretical projection, an average CS Iz of 0.35 triggers
frequent adjustments, yielding an effective ∆Tavg = 0.4◦C (22.4◦C). In the field test, the average CS Iz is 0.28, resulting
in a dampened ∆Tavg = 0.3◦C (22.3◦C) due to system inertia.
To quantify operational benefits relative to the baseline, we define two key metrics: Projected Energy Savings
(PES) and Comfort Improvement Rate (CIR). These metrics correspond directly to the "Energy Consumption" and
"Occupant Dissatisfaction (PPD)" rows in Table D.16, respectively.
1. Projected Energy Savings (PES): Based on ASHRAE handbook and prior studies [2, 7], adjusting the heating
setpoint by 1◦C can reduce heating energy consumption by approximately 3–5%. The PES is calculated by weighting
the potential savings by the model’s recognition accuracy (Acc) and the frequency of behavior detection (Fbeh).
PES = Fbeh · Acc · ηenergy · ∆Tavg
1◦C
(D.3)
where ηenergy ≈4% is the energy saving coefficient per degree, and ∆Tavg is the average effective setpoint adjustment.
For the "Theoretical Projection" column in Table D.16, PES (1.5%) is calculated using Eq. D.3 with Fbeh = 100%,
Acc = 94.2%, and ∆Tavg = 0.4◦C. For the "Actual Field Test" column, energy savings (1.2%) are directly measured
via smart meters, reflecting the real-world impact of system inertia not fully captured in the theoretical PES formula.
2. Comfort Improvement Rate (CIR): Comfort improvement is defined as the reduction in hours where occu-
pants exhibit cold/heat stress behaviors but the HVAC system fails to respond. The baseline strategy has a 0% response
32
rate to behaviors. The proposed strategy’s response rate is bounded by the model’s True Positive Rate (TPR). Thus,
CIR is estimated as follows.
CIR = Hoursbaseline_dissatisfied −Hoursproposed_dissatis fied
Hoursbaseline_dissatis fied
≈Acc · (1 −False_Negative_Rate)
(D.4)
In Table D.16, CIR is quantified as the percentage reduction in Occupant Dissatisfaction (PPD). The baseline
strategy has a 0% response rate to behaviors. The proposed strategy’s response rate is bounded by the model’s True
Positive Rate (TPR). The theoretical CIR corresponds to the 36.7% PPD reduction (15.0% to 9.5%), while the actual
CIR corresponds to the 28.0% PPD reduction (15.0% to 10.8%) observed in field surveys.
It is worth noting that while the field test results validate the positive trend of the theoretical model, certain
deviations are observed. Real-world benefits may vary due to the following factors.
• HVAC System Inertia: Thermal systems have delayed response times, which may reduce the effectiveness of
frequent setpoint adjustments. Thermal systems have delayed response times, which may reduce the effec-
tiveness of frequent setpoint adjustments. The theoretical model assumed instant setpoint adjustments, but the
actual heating system required 15-20 minutes to stabilize after a setpoint change. This delay occasionally led to
minor overshooting, reducing net efficiency.
• Occupant Adaptation Latency: Occupants may not immediately perceive or respond to environmental changes.
Survey feedback indicated occupants did not immediately perceive comfort improvements after the system
adjusted, and some behavioral responses (e.g., putting on clothes) were habitual rather than purely thermal-
driven, leading to occasional false triggers.
• Building Envelope Characteristics: Insulation quality, window-to-wall ratio, and external weather conditions
significantly influence actual energy consumption.
• Model Generalization: Performance may degrade when deployed in buildings with different environmental
conditions or occupant demographics. The recognition and location accuracy in the field is slightly lower than
the controlled test set due to unseen environmental variations.
Future work will involve coupling TLA-DETR with Building Energy Modeling (BEM) tools (e.g., EnergyPlus)
for full-year simulation and field deployment to validate these projected outcomes.
Appendix E. Performance Limitations and Boundary Conditions Across Different Building Environments
While the current study validates TLA-DETR within a controlled commercial office environment in Xi’an, we
acknowledge that deploying this framework in diverse building types (e.g., residential, industrial, or educational) in-
troduces specific challenges. Due to practical constraints, cross-building field testing was not conducted in this phase.
However, to address the requirement to define boundary conditions and expected performance degradation, we provide
a theoretical analysis of key factors that may limit model generalizability across different building environments.
One primary factor influencing performance degradation is thermal background complexity. The current dataset
features relatively uniform office backgrounds. In industrial buildings or atria, complex heat sources, including ma-
chinery, large glass facades, and direct solar gain, may introduce high-frequency thermal noise that exceeds the FMA
module’s suppression capacity. We expect a recognition accuracy degradation of approximately 3–5% in environments
with dynamic thermal clutter. Closely related to background complexity is the architectural layout and potential for
occlusion. The multi-view setup in this study assumes clear lines of sight from three angles. In cubicle-heavy offices,
residential spaces with furniture clutter, or crowded educational halls, occlusion rates may increase significantly.
While the Yager fusion rule handles conflicting views, it cannot recover information from fully occluded subjects. In
high-occlusion scenarios exceeding 40% body occlusion, location accuracy may drop below 80%.
Beyond physical environmental factors, HVAC system characteristics also play a critical role. The frequency of
thermal adaptation behaviors is closely tied to HVAC control logic. In buildings with Radiant Heating or Cooling Sys-
tems, occupants may exhibit fewer convective adaptation behaviors compared to forced-air systems. This distribution
33
shift may affect the model’s confidence calibration, though the core recognition architecture remains valid. Further-
more, although infrared imaging is insensitive to visible light, environmental lighting and solar gain remain relevant.
Direct solar radiation through windows can create hot spots on human bodies that mimic behavioral heat signatures.
In buildings with large window-to-wall ratios, this may increase false positive rates for heat-sensation behaviors.
Based on the current validation and the above analysis, we define the following boundary conditions for reliable
deployment.
• Recommended: Commercial offices, conference rooms, and libraries with controlled HVAC systems, moderate
occupant density (<2 persons/m²), and standard ceiling heights (2.5–3.5 m).
• Caution Required: Residential spaces (high furniture occlusion), industrial workshops (high thermal noise), and
atriums (complex air stratification). Fine-tuning or retraining with local data is strongly recommended for these
scenarios.
• Not Recommended : semi-open spaces, environments with extreme temperature gradients (>10°C difference
within the field of view), or scenarios with persistent heavy occlusion.
To mitigate these limitations in realistic deployments, several strategies may need to be adopted. Domain adap-
tation involves collecting a small subset of local infrared data for fine-tuning the FFN layers to adapt to specific
background thermal signatures. Online learning mechanisms can update the model’s background modeling over time
as seasonal changes affect the building’s thermal envelope. Additionally, in high-noise environments, complementing
infrared data with passive infrared motion sensors can filter out static thermal noise before processing video frames.
This analysis clarifies the operational envelope of TLA-DETR and provides a reference for adapting the framework
to broader building engineering applications beyond the under-controlled office environment and settings validated in
this work.

papers.filename: Light Field Depth Estimation for Non-Lambertian Objects via Adaptive Cross Operator.pdf
papers.text:
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS FOR VIDEO TECHNOLOGY, VOL. 34, NO. 2, FEBRUARY 2024
1199
Light Field Depth Estimation for Non-Lambertian
Objects via Adaptive Cross Operator
Zhenglong Cui , Hao Sheng , Member, IEEE, Da Yang , Sizhe Wang , Rongshan Chen , and Wei Ke
Abstract— Light field (LF) depth estimation is a crucial
basis for LF-related applications. Most existing methods are
based on the Lambertian assumption and cannot deal with
non-Lambertian surfaces represented by transparent objects
and mirrors. In this paper, we propose a novel Adaptive-
Cross-Operator-based(ACO)
depth
estimation
algorithm
for
non-Lambertian LF. By analyzing the imaging characteristics
of non-Lambertian regions, it is found that the difficulty of
depth estimation lies in the photo inconsistency of the center
view. Combining with the two-branch structure, we propose
ACO with an inter-branch cooperation strategy to adaptively
separate depth information with different reflectance coefficients.
We discover that the bimodal distribution feature of the operator
filtering results can assist in the separation of multi-layer scene
information. The first detection branch filters the EPI and
implicitly records the severity of multi-layer scene aliasing.
According to the identification of bimodal distribution features,
the non-Lambertian regions are marked out and the depth of the
foreground is estimated. The second branch receives guidance
from the first to dynamically adjust the inner weight and
infer the background’s depth after weakening the interference
from the foreground. Finally, the depth information separation
of multi-layer scenes is achieved by extracting the unique
X-shaped linear structure. Without the reflection coefficients of
the non-Lambertian object, the proposed method can produce
high-quality depth estimation under the transparency of 90%
to 20%. Experimental results show that the proposed ACO
outperforms state-of-the-art LF depth estimation methods in
terms of accuracy and robustness.
Index Terms— Light field, non-Lambertian, depth estimation,
non-Lambertian region detection, adaptive cross operator.

intensity of light rays on the 2D imaging plane and

loses the direction information of light inevitably. In com-
parison, light field (LF) cameras record both spatial and
angular light ray information in a single shot. This unique
4D dense sampling characteristic provides a new method for
3D scene reconstruction. As a fundamental task in LF image
processing, LF depth estimation has been widely explored in
the last few years. Most works like [1], [2], [3], and [4] are
based on a common Lambertian assumption that one scene
point is observed as the same intensity in different views.
Take it a step further, when fixing an LF image’s spatial
coordinates and refocusing pixels from the correct depth, the
obtained angular patch has photo consistency [5]. However,
the Lambertian assumption is broken where transparent objects
and specular surfaces are present, which results in erroneous
depth estimates.
For Lambertian scenes, photo consistency is the core the-
oretical basis of depth estimation algorithms. Specifically,
correspondence and defocus are two typical costs that are used
to measure photo consistency. Depth estimation algorithms
refocus the angular patches from different candidate depth
labels to compute cost volumes, where the lowest cost and
the highest consistency indicate the selection of the optimal
depth. These Lambertian-assumption-based depth estimation
algorithms perform well in most scenes. On the contrary,
the correspondence and defocus cost generate very blurry
depth estimation in non-Lambertian areas because pixels in
the refocused angular patch may come from a reflector. This
causes existing methods to underestimate their depth or even
wipe out the structures. Accordingly, Lambertian-assumption-
based algorithms generate many artifacts in transparent or
mirror areas, as shown in Fig. 1. Nevertheless, there are
currently limited means of processing for non-Lambertian
region depth estimation. It is even proposed in [7] that ‘We
exclude some training data that contains reflection and refrac-
tion regions, . . .which result in incorrect correspondences.
Reflection and refraction regions were manually masked
out.’ Although [8], [9], and [10] have proposed methods
concentrating on non-Lambertian objects, their performance
is limited by noise and reflectance.
In this paper, we innovatively solve the problem of
depth estimation for scenes with different transparency and
reflectance. We find the X-shaped structure on the EPI as
the key breakthrough. As shown in Fig. 2, the EPI of the
transparent and specular regions exhibit a unique identical
X-shaped structure, while the Lambertian region is simply a
one-way straight-line structure. This difference is due to the
