papers.filename: Rethinking Transparent Object Grasping Depth Completion with Monocular Depth Estimation and Instance Mask.pdf
papers.text:
IEEE
1
Rethinking Transparent Object Grasping: Depth
Completion with Monocular Depth Estimation and
Instance Mask
Yaofeng Cheng, Student Member, IEEE, Xinkai Gao, Sen Zhang, Fusheng Zha,
Chao Zeng Member, IEEE, Lining Sun and Chenguang Yang, Fellow, IEEE
Note: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for pos-
sible publication. Copyright may be transferred without
notice, after which this version may no longer be accessible.
Abstract—Due to the optical properties, transparent objects
often lead depth cameras to generate incomplete or invalid
depth data, which in turn reduces the accuracy and reliability
of robotic grasping. Existing approaches typically input the
RGB-D image directly into the network to output the complete
depth, expecting the model to implicitly infer the reliability of
depth values. However, while effective in training datasets, such
methods often fail to generalize to real-world scenarios, where
complex light interactions lead to highly variable distributions
of valid and invalid depth data. To address this, we propose
ReMake, a novel depth completion framework guided by an
instance mask and monocular depth estimation. By explicitly
distinguishing transparent regions from non-transparent ones,
the mask enables the model to concentrate on learning accurate
depth estimation in these areas from RGB-D input during
training. This targeted supervision reduces reliance on implicit
reasoning and improves generalization to real-world scenarios.
Additionally, monocular depth estimation provides depth context
between the transparent object and its surroundings, enhancing
depth prediction accuracy. Extensive experiments show that our
method outperforms existing approaches on both benchmark
datasets and real-world scenarios, demonstrating superior accu-
racy and generalization capability. Code and videos are available
at https://chengyaofeng.github.io/ReMake.github.io/.
Note to Practitioners—Transparent objects are often misesti-
mated by depth cameras in real-world scenarios due to reflection
and refraction, resulting in unreliable robotic grasps. To address
this, it is essential to accurately recover depth from the input
RGB-D image while ensuring that the method generalizes well
under complex lighting conditions. In this work, we address the
challenge of perceiving transparent objects by combining instance
segmentation and monocular depth estimation. Our method
explicitly identifies transparent regions using an instance segmen-
tation mask and leverages depth context between transparent and
non-transparent regions to guide depth reconstruction. This leads
to more accurate depth perception of transparent objects and
supports robust robotic grasping in real-world scenarios. Our
Yaofeng Cheng, Xinkai Gao, Sen Zhang, Fusheng Zha, and Lining Sun are
with the State Key Laboratory of Robotics and System at Harbin Institute of
Technology. Harbin 150001, China. Fusheng Zha is also at Lanzhou Univer-
sity of Technology. (e-mail: {chengyf, gaoxinkai, zhangsen}@stu.hit.edu.cn;
{zhafusheng, lnsun}@hit.edu.cn).
Chao Zeng and Chenguang Yang are with the Department of Computer
Science. University of Liverpool. L69 3BX Liverpool, U.K. (e-mail: chaozeng,
cyang@ieee.org). Yaofeng Cheng finish this work during the academic visiting
in University of Liverpool sponsored by China Scholarship Council Awards.
method significantly improves depth prediction accuracy from
single-view RGB-D input and increases grasp success rates by
53%-63% across both bird’s-eye and horizontal camera views.
These results demonstrate strong performance and generalization
to real-world conditions. In future work, we aim to tackle the
unique challenges posed by hollow transparent objects, which
continue to hinder reliable robotic perception and manipulation.
Index Terms—Transparent grasping, depth completion, robotic
grasping.
I. INTRODUCTION
RGB Image
Depth Image
Original Points
Complete Depth Image
Grasp Prediction
Robot Grasp
Target Object Points
Fig. 1. Method Overview. We predict a complete depth map from a single
RGB-D image, enabling accurate target object points extraction and 6-DoF
grasp prediction.
T
RANSPARENT objects are ubiquitous in daily life, in-
cluding laboratories, kitchens, hospitals, and factories.
However, widely used depth sensors frequently fail to capture
them accurately. Taking RealSense as an example, the infrared
light emitted by the camera can be refracted or reflected by the
surfaces of transparent objects, leading to incorrect or missing
depth values in certain regions. This makes it difficult to re-
liably grasp such objects. Consequently, accurately predicting
the depth of transparent objects remains a key challenge for
successful robotic grasping.
Early research on transparent object perception has explored
methods to reconstruct depth using multi-view information.
These approaches typically capture RGB images [1]–[3] or
multi-view depth images [4], [5] or both together [6] through
camera motion, and then synthesizing information across
views to recover the object’s actual shape. However, acquiring
and fusing multiple images is time-consuming and often
arXiv:2508.02507v1  [cs.CV]  4 Aug 2025
IEEE
2
impractical in occluded or constrained environments, such as
when an object is placed inside a cabinet.
In contrast, recent work on transparent object grasping has
focused on more practical single-view methods, where an
RGB-D image captured from a fixed viewpoint is input to a
network to predict the complete depth map of the target object
[7]–[14]. In these methods, models are trained on datasets
that include ground-truth depth annotations, and losses are
often computed specifically on transparent regions to improve
reconstruction accuracy. However, we observe that despite
achieving promising results on these datasets, these methods
often fail to generalize well in real-world scenarios, struggling
to adapt to new environments and camera viewpoints.
Our analysis of existing datasets and their usage in prior
methods (see details in Sec. III), reveals that so-called “trans-
parent regions” are not uniform. They include not only
missing-depth areas caused by the absence of reflected infrared
light, but also refractive regions with inaccurate depth values,
as well as normal regions with accurate depth. Although
refractive regions contain depth values, these values are often
invalid due to distortion (see Fig. 2 (b)). Existing approaches
feed an RGB-D image into the network and rely on the
model to distinguish between valid and invalid depth values
using only image information, in order to produce an accu-
rate target depth map. However, because optical phenomena
like refraction and reflection are highly variable, introducing
new objects, scenes, or viewpoints often leads to significant
changes in the appearance of affected regions (see Fig. 2 (c)).
Consequently, relying solely on global image information to
distinguish between valid and invalid depth values becomes
increasingly difficult, leading to poor generalization in existing
methods. This insight motivates us to explore new strategies
that reduce the model’s learning burden by explicitly guiding
depth estimation within transparent regions.
In this paper, we propose a novel framework named Re-
Make (Relative depth and Mask attention guided depth
completion), for completing the depth of transparent objects.
Unlike prior methods such as ClearGrasp [7], OOD [12],
and TransAFF [15], which utilize masks merely to remove
transparent depth values during training, ReMake incorporates
the mask directly into the network input to explicitly indicate
transparent regions. During training, the model learns to asso-
ciate masked areas with depth-ambiguous regions, enabling
it to focus on predicting depth in these regions using the
surrounding RGB-D context. This targeted supervision allevi-
ates the need to infer depth reliability implicitly and enhances
generalization to novel objects and scenes.
Moreover, inspired by the human ability to infer object
depth through relative positioning of objects [16], we introduce
a relative depth map as an additional input channel. We
observe that monocular depth estimation methods capture
meaningful spatial context [17]–[20], which we incorporate to
provide relative depth cues for improved prediction in trans-
parent regions. This provides contextual depth relationships
between the transparent object and its surroundings, enabling
the network to more effectively estimate depth in complex
scenes.
To summarize, our key contributions are as follows:
1) We propose a novel transparent object depth prediction
framework that uses a mask to distinguish transparent re-
gions from the background. This explicit guidance directs
the network’s attention toward estimating depth in these
challenging areas, leading to improved generalization in
real-world scenes.
2) We introduce the use of relative depth maps to assist in
reconstructing the depth of transparent regions by exploit-
ing surrounding depth information, enhancing prediction
accuracy in different backgrounds.
3) We conduct extensive experiments on both datasets and
real-world objects, demonstrating that our method sub-
stantially outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, validat-
ing its accuracy and generalization capabilities.
The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section
II reviews related work on transparent object depth prediction.
Section III analyzes the TransCG dataset and current training
strategies. Section IV presents the details of our proposed
method. Section V describes extensive experiments conducted
on both benchmark datasets and real-world scenarios to eval-
uate the proposed approach. Finally, Section VI concludes the
paper and discusses future directions.
II. RELATED WORKS
Although many existing approaches for transparent object
perception rely on multi-view information captured via camera
motion [1]–[6] or use specialized instruments for precise
measurement [21], [22], such methods are often complex and
difficult to deploy in real-world robotic systems. In contrast,
single-view methods offer greater efficiency and practicality
for broader robotic applications. As this paper focuses on
single-view settings, we review prior work on transparent
object perception, 6-DoF grasping, and monocular depth esti-
mation within the single-view constraint.
A. Transparent Sensing
We first introduce the datasets and describe how they
preserve the original depth of transparent objects. Cleargrasp
[7] is a pioneering work that introduced both real and synthetic
datasets for transparent object reconstruction. It explicitly re-
moved depth values in transparent regions using segmentation
masks to avoid interference from unreliable sensor measure-
ments. The OOD dataset [12] employs a similar strategy, re-
moving depth values in transparent regions during training, and
is frequently used in conjunction with ClearGrasp. TransAFF
[15] also excludes depth information from transparent areas
to mitigate the effects of sensor noise and improve training
stability. However, the TODD dataset [23] later demonstrated
that completely ignoring depth in transparent regions yields
suboptimal results. Similarly, the DREDS [9] and TransCG [8]
datasets advocate for preserving noisy depth in transparent ar-
eas, arguing that it can improve depth prediction performance.
Despite the ongoing debate over whether to retain or discard
depth in transparent regions, existing datasets typically employ
full RGB-D images [7], [8], [12], [15], [23] or a combination
of RGB and IR images [9]. These datasets all aim to recon-
struct complete depth maps. Although these methods calculate

full depth maps, they compute the loss only within trans-
parent regions, encouraging the model to specifically focus
on transparent depth prediction. Subsequent approaches based
on these datasets typically emphasize learning correlations
between RGB information and depth. They leverage cues
such as boundaries and surface normals [7], [10], [24], or
utilize affordance maps [15] to guide depth recovery. Several
methods emphasize architectural innovations. These include
UNet-based models such as DFNet [8], DepthGrasp [10],
and FDCT [11]; pyramid architectures like TCRNet [13];
and transformer-based designs such as SwinDRNet, which
employs Swin Transformer encoders [9], [25]. Additionally,
refinement network modules are used in OAGF [14] and
TDCNet [24]. In contrast, TranspareNet [23] introduces a
different approach: it converts the depth map into a point cloud
for feature extraction, then projects it back into depth space
for final prediction.
In this work, we argue for retaining depth in transparent
regions and provide experimental evidence supporting its
effectiveness. Furthermore, we introduce a novel cue, relative
depth, which enables the network to infer transparent object
depth by learning its depth relationship with surrounding
backgrounds. To support this, we compute the loss over the
entire depth map rather than restricting it to transparent regions
alone.
B. Monocular Depth Estimation
Studies have shown that humans estimate depth by ob-
serving the spatial relationships between objects [16], which
motivates us to explore similar cues for enhancing transparent
object depth estimation. Through our observation, we find
that monocular depth estimation can convert RGB images
into non-measurement depth maps that encode relative depth
information among objects. In this work, we refer to these as
relative depth maps.
Early monocular methods [17], [18] often focused on im-
proving metric accuracy within constrained domains, such
as the NYU [19] and KITTI [20] datasets. However, these
approaches typically suffer from poor generalization when
applied to novel scenes. Recent research has shifted toward
zero-shot depth estimation, aiming to make reasonable depth
predictions without relying on ground-truth annotations. To
improve generalization, some methods treat depth estimation
as a denoising task using diffusion models [26], while oth-
ers scale up training data to enhance robustness [27], [28].
DepthAnything [29] identifies serious deficiencies in exist-
ing real-world depth datasets and instead leverages synthetic
images to train models using pseudo-labels. This strategy
enables broad generalization across diverse object categories
and scenes.
In this work, we do not propose a new monocular depth esti-
mation model. Instead, we adopt the generalizable DepthAny-
thing [29] model to generate relative depth maps. These maps
which are used as auxiliary inputs during training to guide our
network in learning depth relationships between transparent
regions and their surroundings, ultimately improve the depth
estimation accuracy.
C. Robotic Grasping
Grasping is a fundamental step in robotic interaction with
the environment, where the goal is to generate feasible grasp
poses based on the geometry of the target object. Mainstream
approaches can be categorized into 4 degree-of-freedom (DoF)
and 6-DoF grasping. 4-DoF methods [30]–[34] typically gen-
erate grasp rectangles from images, constraining the grasp
orientation to be aligned with the camera view direction, thus
limiting the solution space. With advances in point cloud
processing [35], [36], 6-DoF grasping, where grasp poses are
generated directly in 3D space, has gained popularity due to
its greater flexibility and expanded search space [37]–[42].
To enable object-specific grasp generation, methods such as
ContactGraspNet [39] and PCF-Grasp [42] apply instance
masks to extract the depth of the target object before grasp
prediction. This practice supports the rationale behind incor-
porating instance masks in our transparent object grasping
framework.
IEEE
4
Since single-view perception only captures the visible sur-
face geometry from the camera’s viewpoint, making accurate
grasp generation is inherently challenging. This limitation
becomes even more critical for transparent objects, where
depth measurements are often noisy or missing. Therefore,
recovering accurate depth maps of transparent objects is es-
sential for enabling reliable 6-DoF grasp planning.
III. ANALYSIS OF TRANSPARENT COMPLETION
A. Preliminary
In this paper, we focus on single-view transparent object
depth completion, where the input consists of a monocular
RGB image Ii ∈RH×W ×3 and its corresponding depth image
Di ∈RH×W , and the goal is to predict a complete depth map
Do ∈RH×W for downstream 6-DoF robotic grasping.
To facilitate depth prediction for transparent objects, several
works [7], [8], [23] have constructed large-scale datasets
containing RGB-D images (Ii, Di) captured from real or
simulated environments, along with ground-truth depth maps
Dgt
∈RH×W and binary masks of transparent regions
Mtrans ∈RH×W , as illustrated in Fig. 2 (a).
Some methods [7], [12], [15] regard the depth values in
transparent regions as noisy and unreliable. These values are
thus excluded using a transparency mask Mtrans, resulting in a
masked depth input Dimd. These methods then perform depth
completion by feeding both the RGB image and the masked
depth input into the network, as illustrated below:
Do = f(Ii, Dimd)
(1)
However, recent works [8], [23], [24] have found that retain-
ing the full RGB-D input often leads to better performance. As
a result, most current approaches input the unmodified RGB
and depth images directly into the network, as shown below:
Do = f(Ii, Di)
(2)
Since non-transparent regions already contain valid depth,
their output can be directly retained. As a result, most methods
compute the loss only over transparent regions using a masked
loss, as defined below:
L((Do, Dgt)|Mtrans)
(3)
This masked loss formulation enables the model to focus
on reconstructing depth in transparent regions while excluding
areas with already valid depth values.
B. Dataset Analysis
Although current methods achieve good performance on
existing benchmarks, they often fail to generalize effectively
to unseen scenes. This raises the question: why is their gener-
alization poor? We hypothesize that these methods struggle
to distinguish between valid and invalid depth values. To
investigate this, we analyze and visualize the TransCG dataset
[8].
Transparent sensing can be categorized into three distinct
conditions: 1) Reflection: No reflected light returns to the
camera, resulting in missing depth information. These regions
are typically assigned a depth value of zero; 2) Refraction:
Light passes through a transparent object and returns to the
camera along a distorted path due to refraction, producing
inaccurate depth readings; 3) Normal: Light returns along
a correct path, producing accurate depth measurements. As
shown in Fig. 2 (b), we visualize the depth error between
input depth and ground-truth depth maps in TransCG [8].
Notably, the refraction and normal regions are fundamen-
tally different. Although both provide non-zero depth values,
only the normal regions yield accurate measurements, whereas
refraction results in distorted and unreliable depth. Therefore,
completely removing depth values in transparent regions also
discards valid information, undermining the useful cues that
could otherwise aid in depth prediction.
Moreover, the spatial distribution of these three regions
can vary significantly under different viewpoints, as shown
in Fig. 2 (c). As a result, methods that directly input RGB-D
images into the network require the model to implicitly infer
the reliability of depth values throughout the image. However,
due to the complex and variable behavior of light in real-world
scenes, such models often fail to generalize effectively beyond
their training data.
This analysis suggests that explicitly guiding the model
to identify transparent regions is essential for improving
generalization to unseen environments, rather than relying on
implicit learning alone. To this end, we propose ReMake,
a novel framework that introduces a segmentation mask to
clearly distinguish transparent areas, enabling the model to
focus on reconstructing unreliable depth more effectively.
Additionally, by introducing monocular depth estimation, the
model is guided to capture the relative depth context between
the transparent regions and their surrounding regions, offer-
ing broader spatial understanding than approaches that focus
exclusively on transparent regions.
IV. METHOD
A. Method Overview
In this section, we will introduce the detailed architecture
of the proposed ReMake, as shown in Fig. 3. Our framework
consists of two key components: mask-attention encoding
and the relative depth cue module. We first concatenate the
RGB image with the transparent region mask Mtrans, and
feed it into an encoder to output Fmask, which enhances the
distinction between the transparent object and the background
(Sec. IV-B). Meanwhile, the relative depth map Drel ∈RH×W
generated from monocular depth estimation is input into
another encoder to extract the depth relationship feature Frel
between the transparent region and its surrounding areas,
thereby providing a localization of the transparent object (Sec.
IV-C). The depth image is solely input to an MLP encoder
to extract the depth feature Fdepth. Finally, Fmask, Frel and
Fdepth are combined into a residual structure decoder to
predict the complete depth map Do. The overall framework
is formulated as:
Do = f(Ii, Mtrans, Drel, Di)
(4)

Fig. 3. Overview of the ReMake framework. The instance mask and relative depth map are generated through segmentation and monocular depth estimation,
respectively. The RGB image concatenated with the mask is encoded using a Transformer. The relative depth and original depth are encoded separately. All
encoded features are then fused and decoded to predict the complete depth map. The target object’s point cloud is then extracted using the instance mask.
B. Mask Attention
As shown in Sec. III-B, the depth of transparent regions is
complex, yet it still contains valuable depth cues that can help
depth reconstruction. To reduce the model’s learning burden
while retaining valuable depth cues in transparent regions, we
introduce a mask that explicitly separates these regions from
the background. This enables the model to concentrate on
reconstructing depth within the masked areas by effectively
leveraging both RGB and depth inputs.
To implement this, given an RGB image Ii and depth image
Di, we first concatenate the transparent region mask Mtrans
with the RGB image to form the mask feature Fmask ∈
RH×W ×4, which is passed through a Swin-Transformer [25]
encoder for multi-layer feature extraction, following the design
of TDCNet [24].
C. Relative Depth
Since transparent objects exhibit relative spatial relation-
ships with nearby objects, we propose leveraging relative
depth information to guide the reconstruction of their depth
more accurately. We observe that monocular depth estimation
[17]–[20], [26]–[28] provides non-metric depth maps that
represent relative depth relationships. Notably, these depth
maps lack real-world metrics and cannot be directly used for
grasping tasks. However, they still effectively capture spatial
relationships, which are particularly valuable for estimating
the depth of transparent regions.
As shown in Fig. 3, based on the input RGB image, we
use DepthAnything [29] to generate a relative depth image
Drel. This map is then passed through a Swin Transformer
encoder [25], which extracts multilayer features Frel that are
spatially aligned with the RGB and mask features. Notably, to
preserve the global information of the relative depth map, we
avoid concatenating it with the mask. Instead, it is processed
independently through a separate encoder branch.
D. Loss Function
Since our method infers transparent depth based on spatial
relationships with surrounding objects, globally accurate depth
prediction is crucial. Therefore, unlike previous methods that
apply masked loss only within transparent regions (Eq. 3), we
adopt a global L1 loss computed over all pixels: L(Do, Dgt).
We refer to these two types of supervision as mask loss and
global loss in the following sections.
E. Implementation Details
Training: We applied the TransCG dataset [8] for training,
evaluation, and real-world experiments. The model was trained
with global loss by default. For comparative analysis, we
additionally trained the model using mask loss, following
previous methods [8], [24], [43], and used it for evaluation
and visualization purposes.
Metrics: For depth estimation on the TransCG dataset,
we choose the root mean square error (RMSE), the absolute
relative difference (REL), the mean absolute error (MAE), and
the threshold δ as metrics. It is important to note that results
obtained on the dataset do not always align with real-world
performance, reflecting the challenges of generalization.

Model Implementation: We use an Intel Xeon 6226R CPU
and eight NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPUs for training,
with a single GPU used for testing. Real-world experiments
are conducted on a computer equipped with an AMD 7700X
CPU and a single NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU. We set
the batch size to 8 and the input image size to 640 × 480. We
employ the AdamW optimizer with an initial learning rate of
0.001, which is decayed to one-tenth every 15 epochs over
a total of 40 epochs. The Transformer and MLP encoders
follow the design of TDCNet [24], while the decoder is im-
plemented as a multi-layer residual MLP, inspired by ResNet
[45]. Since our method requires an instance mask as input,
we use Segment Anything [46] for mask generation in real-
world experiments. It is worth noting that any segmentation
algorithm can be used; we select Segment Anything because
it can segment transparent objects in diverse scenes without
retraining. However, it is relatively time-consuming, taking
approximately 2.15 seconds per frame on our real-world setup.
The total processing time per frame is around 2.16 seconds,
with monocular depth estimation requiring 0.0032 seconds and
depth map prediction taking 0.0105 seconds.
V. EXPERIMENT
In this section, we evaluate the proposed method on both
benchmark datasets and real-world grasping experiments,
comparing its performance against state-of-the-art methods.
Finally, we conduct ablation studies to evaluate the contri-
bution of each module within the proposed framework.
A. Dataset Completion Results
We compare our method with state-of-the-art approaches on
the TransCG dataset, and the quantitative results are reported
in Table I. Our model achieves the best overall performance
across evaluation metrics. When trained using loss computed
solely within the masked transparent regions, it obtains the
highest accuracy on the dataset. Using a global loss results in a
slight reduction in performance, though it remains competitive.
In contrast, retraining TDCNet [24] with a global loss causes a
notable degradation in accuracy, indicating that our framework
is more robust to supervision strategy changes.

models such as DFNet [8] and TDCNet [24] exhibit poor generalization to novel views. In comparison, ReMake consistently demonstrates robust performance

To better understand model behavior across different trans-
parent region types, we analyze prediction errors in various
regions. Fig. 4 visualizes the per-pixel error and the average
error for two representative examples. As shown, DFNet [8]
exhibits the highest overall error. TDCNet performs well in
normal regions, but its error increases notably in refractive
and reflective areas. In comparison, our method achieves more
balanced errors across all three region types.
We further report the average error of TDCNet across the
entire dataset in Table III. While TDCNet with masked loss
achieves the lowest error in normal regions, its performance
degrades significantly in refractive areas. This suggests that
the model over-relies on the original depth input, reducing
its robustness in regions where depth is distorted. In contrast,
our method maintains more balanced errors across all region
types, indicating that it learns to treat all masked regions as
potentially unreliable and consistently reconstructs depth using
both RGB-D inputs and contextual cues.
Comparing our global and masked loss settings, we ob-
serve that restricting supervision to masked regions slightly
improves performance on the dataset. However, we adopt the
global loss as our default, since synthetic dataset performance
does not always correlate with real-world results. This decision
is further demonstrated in our real-world ablation experiments
(see Sec. V-C).

imperfect labels. In contrast, our model, informed by relative

B. Real-world Grasping Experiments
1) Experiment Setting: To evaluate our method in real-
world conditions, we use a Franka robot to conduct object
grasping experiments. The grasped objects include: a bottle,
a large tube (tube L, radius 32mm), a small tube tube S,
radius 22mm), a tube filled with water (tube L/w) a beaker, a
wine glass, and a cup, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). The small tube
exhibits stronger refraction effects due to its higher curvature,
while the presence of water in the large tube further alters its
refraction behavior.
To assess the model’s robustness under varying visual
conditions, we test it with both background color variations
and changes in background depth. As shown in Fig. 6 (c), three
camera viewpoints are used: top-down, bird-eye, and horizon-
tal, representing progressively increasing distances between
the background and the object. In the top-down and bird-eye
views, different background textures, including colored paper
and newspapers, are randomly placed beneath the objects.
The horizontal view naturally contains a complex and variable
background due to its viewing angle.
We use PCF-Grasp [42] as the 6-DoF grasp generation
algorithm to generate grasp proposals based on a single-view
depth image. As the grasp algorithm needs a segmentation
mask to choose the target object, we use the same mask as the
model input. Specifically, we employ the Segment Anything
model [46] to generate this mask. For each experiment, a
single object is placed on the table, and grasp proposals are
generated. Each object undergoes seven trials per camera view.
The quantitative grasping results are reported in Table
II. Since the TransCG dataset [8] is collected from a top-
down camera view, existing methods generally perform well
under this setting, though some still exhibit minor distortions
and inaccuracies. However, when the camera viewpoint shifts
to a bird-eye or horizontal, the spatial relationship between
the transparent object and the background becomes more
complex, typically with a greater distance between them,
which significantly increases the difficulty of accurate depth
prediction, as Fig. 7. As a result, the grasp success rate of
previous methods [24], [38] drops sharply under these new
viewpoints. In contrast, our method not only achieves state-
of-the-art performance in the top-down view but also maintains
robust grasp success rates across both bird-eye and horizontal
views. This demonstrates the model’s ability to adapt to chang-
ing backgrounds and varying spatial configurations, thereby
demonstrating stronger generalization capabilities in practical
scenarios.
C. Ablation Study
To show the effectiveness of our modules, we evaluate our
model with the following settings:
• ReMake Blank: ours without mask and relative depth.
• ReMake w/o Rel: ours without relative depth.
• ReMake w/o Mask: ours without a mask.
• ReMake w/o mask depth: removing the mask depth
before inputting to the model [7], [12].
IEEE
10
(a) ReMake 
(b) ReMake (w/o mask)
(c) ReMake (rel encoder)
Fig. 9. Encoder feature map visualization. (a) ReMake with a mask clearly highlights transparent regions. (b) Without a mask, the encoder fails to distinguish
them from the background. (c) The relative depth encoder captures coarse object relations but loses fine-grained depth details.
We conduct training and evaluation on the TransCG dataset
[8], with results summarized in Table IV. We further validate
our method through real-world experiments under various
camera viewpoints, as shown in Fig. 8. To provide a more
complete evaluation, we analyze both the synthetic and real-
world results in combination.
Integrating the instance mask significantly improves predic-
tion accuracy on the TransCG dataset, while models without
the mask exhibit substantial performance degradation in real-
world scenarios. As shown in Fig. 9, incorporating the mask
(Fig. 9 (a)) enables clear separation between transparent ob-
jects and the background. This mask-guided encoding directs
the model’s attention to ambiguous regions, helping it focus
on reconstructing depth where it is most uncertain, thereby
enhancing generalization to real-world scenarios.
Although incorporating relative depth alone contributes to
performance improvements, combining it with the mask leads
to a slight performance drop on the dataset. This is because
relative depth lacks fine-grained geometry details and only
provides coarse depth relationships between objects, as re-
flected in the encoder’s feature maps (Fig. 9 c). As shown in
Fig. 8, relative depth helps maintain prediction accuracy de-
spite dramatic changes in viewpoint and background, thereby
enhancing generalization. Similarly, while using masked loss
alone can achieve better accuracy on the dataset by narrowing
the supervision region (see Table I), it performs poorly in real-
world tests (see Fig. 8) due to its neglect of surrounding depth
context.
We also evaluate the impact of removing depth values in
transparent regions before inputting them into the network. Re-
sults indicate that this approach degrades performance on both
the dataset and in real-world scenarios. This decline occurs
because the removal process eliminates not only unreliable
refracted and reflected depths but also valid depth information
from normal regions, thereby reducing the model’s effective
input and impairing its predictive accuracy.
VI. DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSION
In this work, we propose a novel framework for transparent
object depth completion, guided by relative depth cues and
instance masks. The mask explicitly distinguishes unreliable
transparent regions from background areas, allowing the model
to focus on reconstructing transparent object depth from
RGB-D input during training. Additionally, by incorporating
a relative depth map, the model learns spatial relationships
between transparent and surrounding objects to improve depth
prediction accuracy. Extensive experiments on both benchmark
datasets and real-world scenarios demonstrate the strong gen-
eralization ability and robustness of our method across unseen
scenes and varied camera viewpoints.
Discussion and Limitations:
Our experiments show that removing depth information
from transparent regions degrades prediction accuracy, which
we attribute primarily to the loss of valid depth values
originally present in the raw sensor data. This is further
evidenced by reduced performance in scenes where transparent
objects lack reliable depth entirely. However, it remains un-
clear whether retained refracted depths contribute positively
to reconstruction. A more detailed analysis of these regions
could inform improved depth completion strategies in future
work.
Our model is primarily trained on the TransCG [8] dataset,
where the depth sensor was positioned 50 −70cm from the
target object during data collection. As a result, the model
performs best within this range. Extending the training dataset
to include a wider range of camera-object distances may
improve performance under more diverse settings.
Both the relative depth estimation model and the depth
completion model are generative models and tend to produce
closed surfaces. As a result, they struggle to represent internal
hollow structures of transparent objects-e.g., beakers are often
reconstructed as solid objects. In future work, we plan to ex-
plore representations that better preserve the internal geometry
of transparent objects.

papers.text:
6246
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON IMAGE PROCESSING, VOL. 34, 2025
Mgs-Stereo: Multi-Scale
Geometric-Structure-Enhanced Stereo Matching for
Complex Real-World Scenes
Zhien Dai , Zhaohui Tang , Member, IEEE, Hu Zhang , Senior Member, IEEE,
and Yongfang Xie , Senior Member, IEEE
Abstract—Complex imaging environments and conditions in
real-world scenes pose signiﬁcant challenges for stereo matching
tasks. Models are susceptible to underperformance in non-
Lambertian surfaces, weakly textured regions, and occluded
regions, due to the diﬃculty in establishing accurate matching
relationships between pixels. To alleviate these problems, we
propose a multi-scale geometrically enhanced stereo matching
model that exploits the geometric structural relationships of
the objects in the scene to mitigate these problems. Firstly, a
geometric structure perception module is designed to extract geo-
metric information from the reference view. Secondly, a geometric
structure-adaptive embedding module is proposed to integrate
geometric information with matching similarity information. This
module integrates multi-source features dynamically to predict
disparity residuals in diﬀerent regions. Third, a geometric-
based normalized disparity correction module is proposed to
improve matching robustness for pathological regions in realistic
complex scenes. Extensive evaluations on popular benchmarks
demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance
against leading approaches. Notably, our model provides robust
and accurate predictions in challenging regions containing edges,
occlusions, reﬂective, and non-Lambertian surfaces. Our source
code will be publicly available.
Index Terms—3D reconstruction, depth estimation, stereo
matching, multi-scale feature, geometric-structure perception,
zero-shot generalization.
I. INTRODUCTION
E
STIMATING scene depth plays a critical role in many
computer vision applications [1], [2], [3], [4] (e.g.,
odometry, augmented reality content placement, and robot
Received 11 February 2025; revised 15 June 2025 and 21 August 2025;
accepted 17 September 2025. Date of publication 26 September 2025; date
of current version 2 October 2025. This work was supported in part by
the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under Grant
62171476 and Grant 62376040, in part by the Natural Science Foundation
of Hunan Province under Grant 2023JJ30084, in part by the Key Projects
of NSFC under Grant 62233018, in part by the Key-Area Research and
Development Program of Guangdong under Grant 2021B0101200005, in part
by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation under Grant 2024T171058 and
Grant 2024M753672, and in part by the High Performance Computing Center
of Central South University. The associate editor coordinating the review
of this article and approving it for publication was Prof. Xuejin Chen.
(Corresponding author: Hu Zhang.)
Zhien Dai, Zhaohui Tang, and Yongfang Xie are with the School of
Automation, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China (e-mail:
zhiendai@csu.edu.cn; zhtang@csu.edu.cn; yfxie@csu.edu.cn).
Hu Zhang is with the College of Computer Science and Engineering,
Changsha University, Changsha 410022, China, and also with the School
of Mathematics and Statistics, Central South University, Changsha 410083,
China (e-mail: zhanghu@csu.edu.cn).
Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/TIP.2025.3612754
navigation). For depth estimation, stereo matching is a popular
technology with binocular cameras [5], [6], [7]. It identiﬁes
pixel-wise correspondences between rectiﬁed image pairs and
generates a disparity map of the sense. These maps are
crucial for constructing detailed 3D models, which are pivotal
in advancing research in visualization and computer vision.
These methods enable enhanced rendering realism, improve
simulation accuracy, and facilitate sophisticated data analysis
through immersive visualizations [8], [9].
In recent years, iterative-based stereo matching has become
a popular branch of stereo matching methods. It obtains
matching features from the left and right views through a
feature extractor and then iteratively updates the disparity
map based on a recurrent neural network, such as RAFT-
Stereo [10], Dlnr [11], CREStereo++ [12], and so on. These
models achieve a coarse-to-ﬁne prediction by searching for
more precise matching points in the neighbourhood of the
current disparity result, thereby updating the disparity result
during the iteration process. However, in complex real-world
scenes, occlusions, non-Lambertian surfaces, and textureless
areas lead to low or ambiguous feature similarity. This results
in a correlation volume that is inherently noisy or uninfor-
mative in these regions. Consequently, relying solely on this
compromised information during the iterative process makes
it diﬃcult for the model to converge to an accurate disparity
estimate. As a result, it is diﬃcult to achieve accurate matching
results in these regions, as shown in Fig. 1.
It is well established that humans are capable of perceiving
depth information through a single eye due to their prior
knowledge of geometric structures. With this in mind, our
objective is to learn the geometric structure knowledge of the
scene and utilise it to enhance the robustness of the model’s
performance. Some researchers have proposed the importation
of geometric structural information for stereo matching. They
have adopted a multi-task strategy and attempted to import
the geometric information by predicting the normal map,
segmentation or edge information of the left view [13], [14],
[15], [16].
However, these methods do not suﬃciently account for
the heterogeneity of information across diﬀerent regions in
complex scenes. For instance, in regions with thin object
edges, high-frequency details are essential but vulnerable
to noise degradation. In contrast, in vast areas with little
texture variation, low-frequency information dominates. Fail-
ing to eﬀectively integrate these high and low-frequency

similar technologies. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission.
See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.

DAI et al.: Mgs-Stereo: MULTI-SCALE GEOMETRIC-STRUCTURE-ENHANCED STEREO MATCHING
6247
Fig. 1. Disparity results for challenging regions. The ﬁrst row demonstrates
the partially occluded region due to inconsistent viewing angles, where there
are no strict matching pixel pairs in the occluded region and the model fails to
get the correct disparity results. The second row illustrates the phenomenon of
blurred matching due to weak texture. This occurs when the model propagates
disparity from the edges to the weak texture region, resulting in an incorrect
prediction of disparity in the weak texture region. The third line demonstrates
that within the black box (when the illumination is inconsistent between the
left and right views), the matching conﬁdence between matching points is low
and leads to incorrect matching.
components can lead to confusion between thin structures
and texture-less backgrounds, thereby degrading overall per-
formance. This fundamental challenge arises because their
learned geometric features often fail to form a comprehensive
multi-scale representation of scene geometry. They struggle
with the eﬀective integration of high-frequency components
(e.g., object boundaries) and low-frequency context (e.g.,
planes), as well as the rich contextual relationships between
neighboring pixels. This limitation highlights the need for
a more sophisticated architectural design for the geometric
prior itself. We address this by employing a UNet-based
structure to explicitly integrate multi-scale information, and by
using Neighborhood Attention to aggregate contextual infor-
mation from local neighborhoods, overcoming the limitations
of purely pixel-wise feature processing. This results in a more
holistic and robust geometric prior. Second, regarding the
fusion strategy, their approach is often static. They typically
resort to simple feature concatenation, which lacks the ability
to dynamically balance between photometric matching cues
and the geometric prior. This inﬂexibility makes them prone
to error, as they may either over-smooth textured regions or fail
to regularize ill-posed ones. As a result, they struggle to accu-
rately perceive the complex geometric features of real-world
scenes, and are prone to introducing noise during the disparity
prediction.
In addition, some methods implicitly integrate cross-view
geometric structural information into the cost volume to bol-
ster the model’s geometric awareness [17], [18], [19], [20],
[21], [22], [23]. However, during the construction of the
matching cost matrix, there are instances where no corre-
sponding pixels exist between the left and right views in
problematic areas, such as occluded regions. Moreover, on
non-Lambertian surfaces, the similarity in pixel matching
is notably low. Consequently, during the cost aggregation
phase, the model may struggle to generate accurate disparity
estimates. These limitations can lead to prediction inaccuracies
in speciﬁc regions, such as texture-less areas (e.g., the
background cloth in the second row of Fig. 1), and areas with
inconsistent illumination between views (e.g., the broom’s
handle and the ground behind the grid in the third row of
Fig. 1). Besides, these methods typically focus on the uniform
treatment of global pixels, often neglecting local areas that
require specialized attention, particularly occluded regions. In
these regions, the signiﬁcantly lower pixel ratio compared to
non-occluded areas results in inadequate pixel loss, which
fails to eﬀectively optimize model parameters. This deﬁciency
substantially diminishes prediction accuracy in occluded areas
(e.g., the vertical bar in the ﬁrst row and the covered shelf in
Fig. 1), consequently impairing the overall performance of the
model.
In this paper, we propose a multi-scale geometric-structure-
enhanced stereo matching model (Mgs-Stereo) for complex
real-world scenes from three perspectives. (1) To provide a
robust geometric prior for disparity estimation, especially in
ill-posed regions, we design a Geometric Structure Percep-
tion (GSP) module. The goal of this module is to distill
a uniﬁed geometric representation from the reference image
by explicitly fusing multi-scale structural information. To this
end, it incorporates two key components: a multi-scale fea-
ture alignment block that adaptively combines low-frequency
context (e.g., planar surfaces) with high-frequency details
(e.g., object edges), and a neighborhood attention block
that models local geometric relationships to overcome the
challenge of integrating multi-scale geometric information.
The resulting feature map serves as a strong, explicit geometric
prior that complements the photometric matching signal in
the subsequent stages. (2) To eﬃciently embed the geometric
structural information into disparity prediction tasks, we pro-
pose a geometry structure adaptive embedding module based
on adaptive weighting. This module dynamically adjusts the
weights of geometric and matching similarity features based
on the matching state across diﬀerent areas for adaptive fusion.
Consequently, the model can eﬀectively adapt to disparity pre-
diction in various regions. (3) To address erroneous disparity
predictions in occluded areas, we propose a geometric-based
normalized disparity correction module. The module projects
the disparity map into the normalized space and subsequently
corrects the disparity values of occluded regions using geo-
metric structural features.
In summary, our main contributions are as follows:
• We design a geometric-structure perception module based
on multi-scale feature alignment and neighborhood atten-
tion blocks to learn robust geometric information.
• We propose a geometric-structure adaptive embedding
module that eﬀectively integrates geometric structure
information with matching similarity information.
• We propose a geometric-based normalized disparity cor-
rection module that utilizes geometric structural features
to correct disparity results for pathological regions.
• Our proposed modules achieve competitive performance
on major benchmarks including SceneFlow, KITTI, and
Middlebury. Our method excels in producing accurate dis-
parity results for challenging real-world scenes containing
edges, occlusions, weak textures, and non-Lambertian
surfaces.
Authorized licensed use limited to: BEIHANG UNIVERSITY. Downloaded on November 10,2025 at 07:46:25 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 
6248
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON IMAGE PROCESSING, VOL. 34, 2025
II. RELATED WORKS
Taking advantage of the excellent nonlinear adaptation
capability of neural networks, convolutional neural network
(CNN)-based method has gradually dominated the ﬁeld of
stereo matching [24], [25], [26], [27] recently. Our research
concentrates on iterative-based and geometric structure-guided
stereo matching.
A. Iterative-Based Stereo Matching
Inspired by RAFT, RAFT-Stereo takes an iterative strategy
by multi-layer GRUs [10]. This iterative-based stereo matching
model continuously predicts the disparity residuals through a
recurrent neural network. This iterative optimization not only
saves memory consumption, but also enables depth estimation
to reach a new level of accuracy. Based on the RAFT-Stereo,
CREStereo [28] introduces an adaptive group correlation layer
to mitigate the impact of erroneous rectiﬁcation, and it con-
structs a new synthetic dataset with special attention to diﬃcult
cases for better generalization in real-world scenes. After that,
Dlnr [11] uses a decoupled GRU module for detailed features
with high-frequency during each iteration, and improves the
accuracy of disparity estimation. Additionally, [29] proposes a
iterative update operator that adaptively fuses hidden disparity
information across multiple frequencies for stereo matching.
The above iterative-based stereo matching models attempt
to improve the disparity prediction performance by exploiting
matching similarity information during the iterative process.
In the event of occlusion, the texture-less and the inconsistent
lighting of the real scene result in the absence of matching
points between the views and a low level of matching conﬁ-
dence at the matching points. This may lead to an erroneous
prediction of disparity.
B. Geometric Structure-Guided Stereo Matching
With the geometric structure, the stereo matching model
can predict the depth information of the scene more accurately.
Dovesi et al. [30] propose a real-time semantic stereo matching
network for joint training of semantic segmentation and stereo
matching tasks, which enhances the model’s perception of
the semantic structural information of the scene. Reference
[13] propose a stereo matching method that includes surface
normal prediction. It predicts initial disparity, normal, seg-
mentation, and edge information for the left view, and then
converts surface normal map into a disparity map through
least squares solving. Lac [31] uses local similarity pat-
terns for local geometric structural features to increase the
speciﬁcity of the matched features. IGEV-stereo [17] encodes
geometric and contextual information into cost volume for
enhancing the model’s ability to learn geometric structural
information. To eﬀectively manage intricate structures such
as object boundaries and curved surfaces, [32] utilizes some
local structural information (e.g., slant planes, disparity oﬀset
details, and neighboring relations) to improve stereo matching
in challenging areas. However, these methods are constrained
by two fundamental limitations. First, their geometric feature
extraction often fails to build a comprehensive representa-
tion. They struggle to unify features across diﬀerent spatial
scales, often capturing high-frequency edge details or low-
frequency planar context, but failing to model the cross-scale
relationships between them. Furthermore, these features are
typically processed on a per-pixel basis, lacking a mechanism
to explicitly model the local structural dependencies between
neighboring pixels, thereby failing to build a context-aware
geometric representation. Second, the fusion of these geomet-
ric features with the photometric matching cost is typically
handled by a static mechanism. This static approach prevents
the model from dynamically assessing the reliability of the
matching signal in diﬀerent regions. Consequently, the model
lacks an adaptive embedding strategy to intelligently increase
the inﬂuence of the geometric prior in ill-posed areas while
preserving the integrity of the high-conﬁdence matching cost
in well-textured regions.
Contemporaneous
with
our
work,
another
signiﬁcant
research direction has focused on leveraging large-scale, pre-
trained models to enhance stereo matching, particularly in
ill-posed regions. For instance, Monster [33] and Foundation-
Stereo [34] propose to integrate monocular and stereo depth
estimation by using a powerful pre-trained monocular network
to provide a dense, explicit prior, which then guides the stereo
matching process. In contrast to these approaches that rely
on knowledge from external, pre-trained models, our Mgs-
Stereo instead relies on an internally generated geometric
prior. Our Geometric Structure Perception (GSP) module is
speciﬁcally designed to learn and extract a robust geometric
prior directly from the reference stereo image itself. This prior
is trained end-to-end within our stereo framework, allowing
the model to generate its own context-aware guidance without
external dependencies. This internally-generated prior is then
dynamically integrated by our adaptive fusion module, pro-
viding a complementary strategy for improving robustness in
challenging scenes.
III. MGS-STEREO
As shown in Fig. 2, Mgs-Stereo mainly comprises four
principal components: feature extraction, geometric struc-
ture perception, geometric structure adaptive embedding and
geometric-based normalized disparity correction module.

B. Geometric Structure Perception Module
Based on the UNet architecture, this module integrates
low-frequency structural information with high-frequency
structural information at each scale through a feature align-
ment module. Additionally, we introduce a neighborhood
attention module at each scale to capture the structural depen-
dencies among neighbored pixels, as shown in Fig. 3.
1) Multi-Scale Feature Alignment:

This formulation allows the network to selectively emphasize
ﬁne-grained details in textured areas while relying more on
smoother structural information elsewhere.
2) Neighborhood Attention: To capture geometric structural

C. Geometric Structure Adaptive Embedding Module
In real-world scenarios, achieving precise pixel matching
between left and right views is challenging due to factors like
occlusion and inconsistent lighting. These conditions result in
low matching conﬁdence owing to the less speciﬁc matching
features at these points. To address this, leveraging a prior
geometric structure information in the scene is crucial to com-
plement the matching features. This approach enhances the
model’s accuracy in predicting disparities in such challenging
regions. To eﬃciently fuse geometric structural features with
matching similarity features, we compute adaptive weights
from the adaptive embedding module, as shown in Fig. 4 (a).
First, we calculate the matching state based on the neighbor-
hood matching similarity features Fsim:
ws = Sigmoid(Fstate(Fsim),
(4)
where Fstaterepresents a multi-layer CNN block with a convo-
lutional kernel of size 1, ws represents the learned matching
state weight, a pixel-wise value between 0 and 1 that indi-
cates the conﬁdence in the photometric matching signal
After that, we use the matching state weight to adaptively
fuse the geometric structure features and matching similarity
features:
Fh = Concat{ws · Fgeo, (1 −ws) · Fsim},
(5)
where Concat denotes concatenated operation along the feature
channel.
D. Iterative Update Block
In the kth iteration, we feed the fusing feature Fh to update
the hidden state (hk) of GRU unit:
hk = GRU(hk−1, Fh).
(6)
The hidden state (hk) is then used to predict the disparity
residual (△d) by a CNN block Fdisp, and the disparity result
(dk) can be updated as:
△d = Fdisp(hk)
dk = dk−1 + △d.

IV. EXPERIMENT
A. Dataset and Evaluation Metrics
Datasets Four datesets are included in our experiment:
Scene Flow [36] is a massive stereo synthesis dataset which
contain 35k pairs of training images and 4k pairs of test
images at a resolution of 960×540. We use the endpoint error
(EPE) and the percentage of outlier points (D1) to estimate the
performance of the model. The D1 is deﬁned as the percentage
of points that satisfy |dest −dgt| < 3px or |dest −dgt|/dgt < 5%.
KITTI includes KITTI 2012 [37] and KITTI 2015 [38].
KITTI 2012 is a dataset containing 194 training image pairs
and 195 test image pairs, with a resolution of 1226 × 370.
KITTI 2015 comprises 200 training image pairs and 200 test
image pairs with a resolution of 1242 × 375.
Middlebury [43] dataset is a real-world dataset comprising
15 training pairs and 15 test pairs, with three resolutions: a
full resolution of 3000×2000, a half resolution of 1500×1000
and a quarter resolution of 750 × 500. The Bad2.0 algorithm
is employed.
ETH3D [44] is a collection of grayscale stereo image pairs,
comprising 27 training image pairs and 20 test image pairs,
which have been created for the purpose of training a computer
vision algorithm. The images have been captured in both
indoor and outdoor settings.
Metric We use the endpoint error (EPE) and the percentage
of outlier points (D1) to estimate the performance of the
model. The D1 is deﬁned as the percentage of points that
satisfy |dest −dgt| < 3px or |dest −dgt|/dgt < 5%. The following
metrics are calculated in the paper: the percentage of outliers
with absolute error larger than n pixels (Bad n.0); the root-
mean-square disparity error in pixels (RMS); the 95-percent
error quantile in pixels (A95); and the average absolute error
in pixels (AvgErr).
B. Implementation Details
The framework is implemented with PyTorch. During the
experiment, AdamW is used as the optimizer. The model is
trained with RTX 3090 GPUs and a batch size of 4. For Scene
Flow, the network is trained from scratch for 200,000 steps
with an initial learning rate of 0.0001. Images are randomly
cropped to a size of 384 × 768 pixels. During the testing
phase, full-size images of the same dimensions are used to
estimate the disparity of the left image. To rigorously evaluate
the eﬀectiveness and generalizability of our proposed modules,
we integrate them into two distinct, state-of-the-art iterative
stereo matching backbones: RAFT-Stereo and Selective-IGEV.
This results in two model variants for our experiments, which
we refer to as Mgs-RAFT and Mgs-Selective, respectively.
C. Ablation Studies
We conducted a comprehensive ablation study on the Scene
Flow dataset to validate the eﬀectiveness of each component
of our proposed Mgs-RAFT, with results detailed in Table I.
We start from a baseline model (a), which is a modiﬁed
RAFT-Stereo with a MobileNetV2 backbone. First, we intro-
duce our Geometric Structure Perception (GSP) module. As
shown in row (b), even without its dedicated geometric loss,
the GSP architecture provides a slight improvement, reducing
the EPE to 0.56. This suggests the inherent beneﬁt of the
feature extraction design. Next, by incorporating the geometric
perceptual loss (Lgeo) for explicit supervision (c), we observe a
signiﬁcant performance boost, with the EPE dropping sharply

Building on this, we add the Geometric Structure Adaptive

Finally, we evaluate the Normalized Disparity Correction

specialized loss (e), the performance sees a marginal gain,

loss function to eﬀectively optimize the model for challenging
occluded regions. Overall, the ablation study systematically
demonstrates the individual and collective contributions of
each proposed module and its associated loss function. The
qualitative result in Fig. 5 (a), where the model accurately
predicts the disparity in the occluded region behind the guitar,
visually corroborates these quantitative ﬁndings.

We investigate the performance of our model against other
methods on four popular benchmarks.

Flow test set are shown in Table II. Our Mgs-RAFT model
achieves a strong EPE of 0.46, demonstrating a signiﬁcant
improvement over its baseline and outperforming methods
like IGEV-Stereo. More importantly, to demonstrate the gen-
eralizability and eﬀectiveness of our modules on a stronger
foundation, we integrated them into the Selective-IGEV
backbone. The resulting Mgs-Selective model achieves state-
of-the-art performance with an EPE of 0.39. This represents a
substantial 11.4% reduction in error compared to the original
Selective-IGEV and surpasses all other listed methods. As
can be observed from the occluded region situated behind
the guitar in Fig. 5 (a), our approach accurately predicts the
disparity.

training set using a crop size of 384 × 768. The quantitative
results, presented in Table III, highlight the eﬀectiveness and
generalizability of our proposed modules. Our Mgs-RAFT

our models exclusively on the synthetic Scene Flow dataset

improvement over Selective-IGEV (1.43). This validates the
eﬀectiveness of our specialized modules, particularly the
Normalized Disparity Correction (NDC) module, which is
explicitly designed to infer and reﬁne disparity in these areas
where direct matching cues are absent.
TABLE IX
RESULTS OF THE REFLECTIVE REGIONS ON KITTI 2012 LEADERBOARD.
THE BEST RESULTS ARE MARKED IN BOLD
9) Reﬂective (Non-Lambertian) Surfaces: Finally, to val-
idate robustness on non-Lambertian surfaces, we report
performance on the reﬂective regions of the KITTI 2012
benchmark in Table IX. Our Mgs-Selective model again
demonstrates strong performance, reducing the Out-4 (All)
error to 1.99%, compared to 3.05% for Selective-IGEV. The
substantial margin of improvement supports our claim of
enhanced robustness in real-world driving scenes containing
challenging reﬂective surfaces like wet roads or car bodies.
Collectively, these targeted evaluations provide strong quan-
titative evidence that our proposed modules bring tangible
beneﬁts to the exact ill-posed regions that motivated our work.
10) Qualitative Robustness in Diverse Conditions: Beyond
quantitative metrics on speciﬁc regions, it is crucial to assess
a model’s qualitative performance under the diverse and often
adverse conditions encountered in the real world. We con-
ducted a visual robustness analysis across various challenging
weather and lighting scenarios.
As shown in Fig. 8, we compare our Mgs-Selective against
three strong competitors. This visual evidence corroborates
our quantitative ﬁndings and highlights the practical beneﬁts
of our geometric-structure-enhanced approach. For instance,
the superior handling of specular reﬂections on the car in
the sunny scene (a) and on the wet road in the rainy scene
(d) can be attributed to our GASE module, which learns
to rely more on the stable geometric prior (Fgeo) when the
photometric matching signal is corrupted by view-dependent
eﬀects. Similarly, the ability to reconstruct smooth, coherent
surfaces on the low-texture underpass (b) and the foggy
bus side (c) demonstrates the GSP module’s eﬀectiveness in
capturing large-scale geometric context where local texture is
absent. Finally, the preservation of thin structures like the
traﬃc light pole and overhead sign (d) showcases the model’s
precision, preventing the over-smoothing artifacts common in
less robust methods.
Authorized licensed use limited to: BEIHANG UNIVERSITY. Downloaded on November 10,2025 at 07:46:25 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 
6256
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON IMAGE PROCESSING, VOL. 34, 2025
Fig. 8. Qualitative comparison demonstrating the robustness of Mgs-Stereo across diverse and challenging real-world weather conditions. We compare our
Mgs-Selective against RAFT-Stereo, IGEV, and Selective-IGEV under four scenarios: (a) Sunny, (b) Cloudy, (c) Foggy, and (d) Rainy. Our method consistently
produces more accurate and geometrically coherent disparity maps, particularly in regions with challenging properties like non-Lambertian reﬂections, low
texture, and ﬁne structures, which are exacerbated by adverse weather. Speciﬁcally: In the sunny scene (a), Mgs-Selective successfully handles the specular
reﬂections on the car (black box) and preserves the ﬁne details of the distant foliage (yellow box). Under cloudy conditions (b), our model accurately
reconstructs the geometry of the low-texture underpass (red box). In the foggy scene (c), Mgs-Selective generates a remarkably smooth and planar disparity
for the large, weakly-textured bus side (white box). In the rainy scene (d), our method shows superior performance on thin structures like the overhead sign
(white box) and traﬃc light pole (black box), while better handling reﬂections on the wet road (yellow box).
Collectively, these visual results provide compelling evi-
dence that our proposed modules enable the stereo network to
be not only accurate on standard benchmarks but also maintain
a high level of performance and geometric consistency across a
wide spectrum of challenging, real-world imaging conditions.
V. LIMITATIONS AND FUTURE WORK
First, specular (mirror-like) surfaces violate the brightness
constancy assumption by creating view-dependent appear-
ances. As illustrated in Fig. 9(a), the mirror reﬂects diﬀerent
parts of the scene to each camera, leading to severe matching
ambiguity. Consequently, our model attempts to reconstruct
the virtual 3D scene visible within the reﬂection, instead of
estimating the correct, ﬂat disparity of the mirror’s surface
itself. Although our GASE module is designed for low-
conﬁdence areas, the strong, structured (but incorrect) features
from reﬂections can be misinterpreted as high-conﬁdence
matches, leading to failure.
Second, transparent objects break the assumption of
opaque surfaces, as light rays pass through them from the
background. In Fig. 9(b), our model fails to perceive the glass
door. Instead, it “sees through” it and incorrectly matches the
pixels corresponding to the background plants. This results in
the disparity of the background being erroneously assigned
to the foreground transparent object. Our GSP module also
struggles in this case, as the perceived geometric structure is
that of the distorted background rather than the object itself.
Third, our model’s performance can degrade in regions with
an extreme lack of informative cues. The dark shadow in
Fig. 9(c) represents a worst-case scenario, featuring a simulta-
neous absence of both photometric information (texture) and
discernible geometric features. Lacking any reliable signal to
guide the process, our model resorts to erroneous disparity
propagation from the surrounding, better-deﬁned areas, result-
ing in a smooth but incorrect surface.
These limitations point to several exciting avenues for future
research. To directly address the challenges of transparency
and specularity, a promising direction is to explicitly model
material properties. Incorporating a material-aware branch
could allow the model to switch to alternative reasoning
Authorized licensed use limited to: BEIHANG UNIVERSITY. Downloaded on November 10,2025 at 07:46:25 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 
DAI et al.: Mgs-Stereo: MULTI-SCALE GEOMETRIC-STRUCTURE-ENHANCED STEREO MATCHING
6257
Fig. 9.
Visualization of failure cases where Mgs-Stereo struggles due to
violations of fundamental stereo vision assumptions. The top row shows the
left view, and the bottom row shows our predicted disparity. From left to
right: (a) Specular Mirror: The mirror creates view-dependent reﬂections,
violating brightness constancy and causing severe matching ambiguity. The
model attempts to reconstruct the virtual scene within the reﬂection rather
than the ﬂat mirror surface. (b) Transparent Object: The glass door violates
the assumption of an opaque surface. Our model “sees through” the glass and
incorrectly assigns the disparity of the background (the plants outside) to the
foreground object. (c) Extreme Low-Light Region: The dark shadow repre-
sents a worst-case scenario with a simultaneous absence of both photometric
(texture) and geometric cues, leading to erroneous disparity propagation from
surrounding areas.
strategies in these regions. Furthermore, leveraging informa-
tion from multi-view stereo (MVS) or video sequences
could help resolve these ambiguities, as the way reﬂections
and refractions change across multiple viewpoints provides
powerful cues about the underlying 3D geometry.
More broadly, to address scenarios lacking explicit low-level
cues, one could explore incorporating higher-level semantic
scene understanding. For instance, recognizing a surface as
a ‘wall’ or a region as a ‘shadow’ could allow the model to
apply strong geometric priors (e.g., planarity or co-planarity
with the underlying surface). To make the fusion process more
robust against misleading matching signals, future work could
focus on developing more sophisticated, uncertainty-aware
fusion mechanisms for the GASE module, allowing it to
not only assess matching conﬁdence but also the reliability
of that conﬁdence score itself. Finally, from a practical stand-
point, exploring lightweight versions of the GSP module
could improve the eﬃciency of our method for real-time
applications.
VI. CONCLUSION
In this paper, we propose a convolutional neural network
architecture for stereo matching, called Mgs-Stereo. We design
three key modules (i.e., GSP, GASE and NDC) to enhance the
geometric-structure information for stereo matching. The GSP
acquires the geometric structure prior information of the scene,
while the GASE can adaptively fuse the geometric structure
information with the matching similarity information, and the
NDC can correct the disparity of the occluded region. By
incorporating the GSP, our proposed method can learn robust
geometric structure features to improve the model’s matching
robustness in complex real-world scenarios with inconsistent
left and right views (light imbalance, view occlusion, non-
Lambertian surfaces, etc.). In addition, by using the GSAE,
our method can dynamically adjust the weights between the
geometric structure features and the matching similarity fea-
tures according to the matching state, and improve the
robustness of the model by using the geometric structure
information to more favourably adjust the disparity in the
pathological region. By using the NDC module, the model
can be made to further adjust the disparity values of the
occluded regions according to the geometric structural features
of the scene, correcting the incorrect matching results. Our
experiments are implemented on four benchmark datasets (i.e.
Scene Flow, KITTI and Middlebury with ETHD). Experimen-
tal results show that our proposed modules provide signiﬁcant
and consistent improvements to strong stereo matching frame-
works, leading to competitive performance on challenging
benchmarks.

papers.text:
A Taxonomy and Evaluation of Dense Light Field Depth Estimation Algorithms
Ole Johannsen1,a, Katrin Honauer2,a, Bastian Goldluecke1,a,
Anna Alperovich1, Federica Battisti3, Yunsu Bok4, Michele Brizzi3, Marco Carli3,
Gyeongmin Choe5, Maximilian Diebold2, Marcel Gutsche2, Hae-Gon Jeon5,
In So Kweon5, Jaesik Park6, Jinsun Park5, Hendrik Schilling2, Hao Sheng7,
Lipeng Si8, Michael Strecke1, Antonin Sulc1, Yu-Wing Tai9, Qing Wang8,
Ting-Chun Wang10, Sven Wanner11, Zhang Xiong7, Jingyi Yu12, Shuo Zhang7, Hao Zhu8
1University of Konstanz, Germany
2Heidelberg University, Germany
3Roma Tre University, Italy
4ETRI, Republic of Korea
5KAIST, Republic of Korea
6Intel Visual Computing Lab, USA
7Beihang University, China
8Northwestern Polytechnical University, China
9Tencent, China
10UC Berkeley, USA
11Lumitec, Germany
12ShanghaiTech University, China
a contact@lightfield-analysis.net
The ﬁrst two authors contributed equally.
Abstract
This paper presents the results of the depth estima-
tion challenge for dense light ﬁelds, which took place at
the second workshop on Light Fields for Computer Vision
(LF4CV) in conjunction with CVPR 2017. The challenge
consisted of submission to a recent benchmark [7], which
allows a thorough performance analysis. While individual
results are readily available on the benchmark web page
http://www.lightfield-analysis.net, we take this
opportunity to give a detailed overview of the current par-
ticipants. Based on the algorithms submitted to our chal-
lenge, we develop a taxonomy of light ﬁeld disparity esti-
mation algorithms and give a report on the current state-of-
the-art. In addition, we include more comparative metrics,
and discuss the relative strengths and weaknesses of the al-
gorithms. Thus, we obtain a snapshot of where light ﬁeld
algorithm development stands at the moment and identify
aspects with potential for further improvement.
1. Introduction
Over the last decade, light ﬁeld analysis has grown from
a niche topic to an active and established part of the com-
puter vision community. The key difference to the classi-
cal multi-view scenario is the dense and regular sampling,
which allows to develop novel and highly accurate methods
for depth reconstruction which can correctly take occlusions
into account to recover ﬁne details. In recent years, a vari-
ety of algorithms was published [9, 11, 12, 16, 19, 20, 23,
25, 27, 29], but objective comparison of their strengths and
weaknesses is not straight-forward.
While the HCI Light Field Benchmark by Wanner et
al. [26] provided several popular data sets and could be con-
sidered a default for testing in the past three years, eval-
uation on it was not standardized with respect to which
metrics and datasets should be included in the evaluation.
To establish a comparable performance analysis, Honauer,
Johannsen et al. [7] introduced a novel benchmark with
synthetic light ﬁeld data and a comprehensive evaluation
methodology. They presented an evaluation of ﬁve algo-
rithms as a baseline but their focus was on validating the
proposed scenes and metrics.
In order to capture and analyze a representative state-of-
the-art, we initiated the light ﬁeld depth estimation chal-
lenge as part of the second workshop on Light Fields for
Computer Vision (LF4CV), held at CVPR 2017. The chal-
lenge was open to submissions of novel as well as al-
ready published light ﬁeld methods. Challenge participants
submitted their estimated disparity maps and runtimes for
the four stratiﬁed and eight photorealistic scenes on ﬁg-
ure 1. Ground truth is unknown for four of the photorealis-
tic scenes, the other scenes can be used to train parameters,
which however have to be the same for all scenes.
In this paper, we present the results of the depth estima-
tion challenge. With seven challenge participants, two ad-
ditional benchmark submissions, and ﬁve baseline submis-
1 82
Figure 1. The 12 benchmark scenes used for evaluation: four stratiﬁed and four photorealistic training scenes with publicly available ground
truth and four test scenes with hidden ground truth.
Ω image plane
Π focal plane
s
x
Z
X
P=(X,Y,Z)
f
0
Figure 2. Light ﬁeld coordinates and projection. Note that by con-
vention, we parameterize (x, y)-coordinates relative to the princi-
pal point of a pinhole camera located at (s, t).
sions, we analyze a total of 14 algorithms. We believe this
to be a good basis for a taxonomy and thorough evaluation
of the current state-of-the-art. In line with [7], the aim is not
to identify a single best algorithm for the entire benchmark
or for all light ﬁeld data sets - indeed, we believe this is not
possible. Instead, we try to compare relative strengths and
weaknesses of different approaches in order to learn which
components lead to good results for certain scene properties
or structures, and to learn for which scenarios there is still
room for improvement.
2. Light ﬁeld depth estimation
In this section, we will give a short introduction to the
structure of light ﬁelds and brieﬂy review possible strategies
to infer depth. These can roughly be classiﬁed according to
the different representations of the light ﬁeld they rely upon,
and thus give one category for a taxonomy of algorithms.
For the purpose of this paper, we understand a 4D light
ﬁeld as the radiance function sampled on a space of rays.
This 4D ray space is parameterized by the two intersection
points of each ray r with two different planes. The image
plane Ωis parameterized in p = (x, y) coordinates, while
the focus plane Π is parameterized in c = (s, t) coordinates.
Both planes are parallel to each other. Thus, the 4D light
ﬁeld is a function
L : Ω× Π →R,
(x, y, s, t) 7→L(x, y, s, t) = L(p, c).
(1)
In practice, it often has several components, i.e. takes values
in RGB color space R3.
Subaperture views and disparity. The light ﬁeld can
be resampled into several popular representations, see also
Levoy [13] for a more fundamental introduction into light
ﬁeld principles and parameterizations. In the maybe most
intuitive representation, we ﬁx (s, t) coordinates. If (x, y)
coordinates vary, we obtain for each pair (s, t) an image
I(s,t) as captured by an ideal pinhole camera. These cam-
eras have parallel optical axes orthogonal to the planes and
identical focal length, corresponding to the distance be-
tween the planes. The pinhole views obtained in this way
are called subaperture images (see ﬁgure 3).
Let a 3D scene point be at a distance Z from the focal
plane, then the coordinates of its projections in the subaper-
ture views follow the pinhole projection. Two different rays
r1, r2 passing through this point thus are related by
L(p2, c2) = L(p1 −f

It relates all rays emanating from a single point in space
whose radiance is captured in the light ﬁeld. Thus, if this
3D point lies on the scene surface and the scene is Lamber-
tian, all those rays should share the same radiance. This as-
sumption is the basis for all disparity estimation algorithms
discussed in this paper, and it is exploited to infer depth.
The disparity correspondence relation (2) gives rise to
multi-view stereo matching methods, for which there is a
vast literature [17]. Indeed, it implies the intensity relation-
